title: What Causes Blocked Drains in New Zealand Homes?
excerpt: Learn the common causes of blocked drains in New Zealand homes, the warning signs to watch for, and when drain cleaning or repair is needed.
content:
Blocked drains usually develop when waste builds up inside a pipe or when the drain itself becomes damaged. Kitchen fat, wet wipes, hair, tree roots, foreign objects and collapsed pipework are common causes. The correct solution depends on where the blockage sits and whether the pipe remains structurally sound.

A plunger may clear a minor blockage close to a sink or toilet. Recurring blockages, sewage overflows and multiple slow fixtures usually indicate a deeper drainage problem. These cases may require professional [blocked drain unblocking](/blocked-drain-unblocking), CCTV inspection or drain repair.

## 1. Fats, oils and food waste

Cooking fats and oils often enter a kitchen drain as warm liquids. They cool inside the pipe and stick to its inner surface. Food particles then collect on the greasy layer, gradually reducing the open diameter of the drain.

The early signs include:

- A kitchen sink that drains slowly
- Gurgling from the sink waste
- An unpleasant smell near the plughole
- Water backing up into a second sink bowl
- A blockage that returns after basic cleaning

Hot water alone does not reliably remove a developed fat blockage. It may shift softened grease farther along the line, where it cools and hardens again. A severe deposit may require mechanical cleaning or [hydro jetter drain cleaning](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning).

Cooking oil should be allowed to cool and placed in a suitable waste container. Plates and pans should be scraped before washing. Sink strainers can catch larger food scraps before they enter the waste pipe.

## 2. Wet wipes and sanitary products

Toilet paper is designed to break down in water. Wet wipes, paper towels, nappies and sanitary products are not. These materials can remain intact, catch on pipe joints and combine with grease or other waste.

Items that should not be flushed include:

- Wet wipes, including products labelled as flushable
- Paper towels and facial tissues
- Nappies
- Sanitary pads and tampons
- Cotton buds and cotton pads
- Dental floss
- Condoms
- Cat litter

A toilet that blocks shortly after one of these items is flushed may have a local obstruction. If toilets, showers and floor wastes are affected together, the blockage may be in the main wastewater lateral.

## 3. Hair and soap residue

Hair commonly blocks shower, bath and basin wastes. Loose strands collect around strainers, trap components and rough sections of pipe. Soap residue, skin oils and cosmetic products bind the strands into a dense mass.

Water may initially drain only slightly slower than normal. The flow then worsens as more material attaches to the obstruction. Removing visible hair from the waste opening can help, but pushing tools deep into the drain can damage fittings or compact the blockage.

A removable hair catcher provides cheap prevention. Regularly cleaning the shower grate and accessible trap also reduces buildup.

## 4. Tree-root intrusion

Tree roots do not usually break into a completely sound drain. They are attracted to moisture escaping through a crack, failed joint or damaged section. Once inside, fine roots grow into a larger mass that catches paper and solid waste.

Root intrusion can cause:

- Repeated blockages in the same drain
- Slow drainage across several fixtures
- Gurgling toilets
- Sewage overflowing from an external gully trap
- Damp or unusually green ground above the pipe route

Cutting roots can restore flow, but it does not repair the opening that allowed them to enter. The blockage may return unless the defective section is repaired, replaced or assessed for [trenchless pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless).

A [CCTV drain camera inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection) can show the location and extent of root intrusion. It can also reveal cracked pipes, displaced joints and other defects hidden underground.

## 5. Cracked, displaced or collapsed drains

Older drains can deteriorate through ground movement, corrosion, poor installation, heavy loads and normal material ageing. A pipe may crack, deform, separate at a joint or partially collapse. Soil and stones can then enter the line while wastewater escapes into the surrounding ground.

Drain cleaning cannot permanently fix a structural failure. It may create temporary flow through the damaged area, but the obstruction is likely to return. CCTV footage helps distinguish a removable blockage from a pipe that requires [drainlaying and drain repair](/drainlaying-repairs) or [sewer line replacement](/sewer-line-repair).

Warning signs of structural damage include recurring blockages, ground subsidence, persistent sewage smells and unexplained wet patches outside.

## 6. Foreign objects

Small toys, excessive toilet paper, cleaning cloths, food packaging and fragments of damaged fittings can obstruct a drain. Toilets are particularly vulnerable because an object may pass through the bowl but become lodged farther along the trap or pipe.

Repeated flushing can push the object deeper and cause contaminated water to overflow. Stop using the affected fixture if the water level rises instead of falling.

## 7. Poor drain gradient or installation

Wastewater drains rely on a suitable gradient. A pipe that is too flat may allow solids to settle. A pipe that is poorly supported can sag and create a low section where water and waste collect. Incorrect bends, poorly finished joints and undersized pipework can also restrict flow.

These problems may exist from the original installation or develop after ground movement. Cleaning provides limited benefit when the underlying geometry remains wrong. A drainlayer may need to inspect levels, locate the defective section and determine whether alteration or replacement is required.

## 8. Heavy rain and stormwater problems

Stormwater and wastewater serve different purposes. Stormwater carries rain from roofs, channels and outdoor surfaces. Wastewater carries discharge from toilets, showers, basins, kitchens and laundries.

Leaves, silt, rubbish and plant growth can block stormwater drains. Intense rain may then expose an existing restriction by causing gutters, surface drains or downpipes to overflow. Damaged or incorrect connections can create more serious network problems.

New Zealand Building Code guidance requires gully traps to be arranged to limit the entry of surface water and foreign material that could cause a blockage. A roof downpipe should not simply discharge into a wastewater gully trap. Suspected cross-connections should be investigated by an appropriately qualified tradesperson.

Problems involving roof drainage may require [gutter and downpipe installation or repair](/gutter-downpipe-installation). Underground stormwater defects may require [stormwater drain repair](/stormwater-drain-installation).

## 9. Mineral and sediment buildup

Sand, silt, construction debris and mineral deposits can reduce flow through certain drains. Sediment commonly enters outdoor drainage through damaged grates, unsealed openings or surface runoff. Building materials such as grout, plaster and concrete products should never be washed into a drain because they can settle and harden.

A camera inspection can identify where sediment is accumulating. The pipe may need cleaning, but the entry point must also be corrected to prevent another blockage.

## Signs that the main drain may be blocked

A problem affecting one fixture is often close to that fixture. A main drainage blockage normally produces broader symptoms.

Common signs include:

- Several fixtures draining slowly at the same time
- A toilet gurgling when a sink, bath or washing machine empties
- Water rising in a shower or floor waste when a toilet is flushed
- Wastewater overflowing from an external gully trap
- Sewage smells from multiple drains
- Repeated blockages after plunging or basic cleaning

Stop using toilets, taps, showers, dishwashers and washing machines if wastewater is overflowing. Every additional discharge adds water to the blocked system.

## Why a drain keeps blocking after it has been cleared

A recurring blockage normally means the first clearance removed the immediate obstruction without resolving its cause. Common underlying defects include:

- Tree roots entering through a cracked joint
- A partially collapsed pipe
- A sagging section that retains waste
- Heavy grease remaining on the pipe wall
- An object still lodged inside the line
- An incorrect gradient

CCTV inspection is more useful than repeatedly clearing an unknown obstruction. It provides evidence of the drain's internal condition and supports a targeted repair decision.

## Who is responsible for a blocked drain?

Responsibility depends on the blockage location and the rules of the relevant council or water authority. Property owners are commonly responsible for private drains and wastewater laterals serving their property, but the exact boundary of responsibility is not identical across New Zealand.

The cause should be located before assuming that either the owner or council must pay. A drain inspection can help establish whether the problem is inside private pipework, in a private lateral or within the public network. Contact the local council or water authority when the blockage appears to involve a public main.

## Can homeowners clear a blocked drain themselves?

A plunger or removal of visible debris may resolve a minor, accessible blockage. Chemical drain cleaners can expose users to corrosive substances, damage some materials and create a hazard for the person who later opens the drain. Mixing drain-cleaning chemicals is dangerous.

Professional assessment is appropriate when:

- Sewage is overflowing
- Multiple fixtures are affected
- The blockage keeps returning
- Tree roots or pipe damage are suspected
- The affected drain is underground
- Drain repair or alteration may be required
- The blockage involves commercial premises

New Zealand regulates plumbing, gasfitting and drainlaying work. Restricted work must be handled by a person authorised for the relevant trade. Clearing an accessible obstruction is not the same as altering or repairing sanitary drainage.

## How blocked drains are diagnosed

The appropriate method depends on the symptoms and pipe layout. A drainage professional may use:

1. **Fixture and gully-trap checks:** These help establish whether the problem affects a single waste pipe or the main drain.
2. **Mechanical drain clearing:** A cable or cutting head can break through certain obstructions and roots.
3. **High-pressure water jetting:** Water pressure removes grease, sludge and loose deposits from the pipe wall.
4. **CCTV inspection:** A drain camera identifies roots, cracks, displaced joints, deformation and retained objects.
5. **Drain location and testing:** These methods help map buried pipework and investigate leaks or incorrect connections.

Clearing restores flow. Diagnosis establishes why the blockage happened. Both may be necessary when the problem is recurrent.

## How to reduce the risk of another blockage

- Put only human waste and toilet paper into the toilet
- Keep fats, oils and food scraps out of kitchen drains
- Use sink and shower strainers
- Remove leaves from gutters and outdoor drain grates
- Do not wash grout, plaster, paint or concrete residue into drains
- Investigate recurring slow drainage before a complete blockage develops
- Obtain a CCTV inspection when roots or structural damage are suspected
- Use an authorised drainlayer for restricted drainage work

Early action usually limits wastewater damage and makes the source easier to identify. A drain that repeatedly blocks is not functioning normally and should not be treated as a routine inconvenience.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is the most common cause of a blocked household drain?

The cause depends on the fixture. Kitchen drains commonly collect fats and food waste. Shower and basin wastes collect hair and soap residue. Toilets block because of wipes, sanitary products, excessive paper or foreign objects. Underground drains can block because of roots and structural damage.

### Will boiling water clear a blocked drain?

Boiling water may soften a small amount of grease, but it will not repair roots, broken pipework or a solid obstruction. Very hot water can also affect some fittings and create a burn risk. Recurring or complete blockages require proper diagnosis.

### Why does my toilet gurgle when the shower drains?

The two fixtures may share a restricted drain. Moving wastewater displaces air, which can produce bubbles or gurgling through a nearby trap. Multiple affected fixtures are a warning sign of a blockage deeper in the system.

### Does a drain camera clear the blockage?

No. A CCTV camera inspects the drain. Mechanical equipment or water jetting clears the obstruction. Camera footage is useful for locating the cause and checking the pipe after cleaning.

### Should recurring tree roots be cut out again?

Root cutting can restore flow temporarily. Repeated root growth indicates an opening in the drain. Repair, replacement or relining may be needed to prevent another intrusion.

### When should the council be contacted?

Contact the relevant council or water authority when several neighbouring properties are affected, a public manhole is overflowing or evidence indicates a blockage in the public network. Responsibility for wastewater laterals varies between local authorities, so confirm the local policy.

title: How to Tell If Your Main Sewer Drain Is Blocked
excerpt: Learn the warning signs of a blocked main sewer drain, how to distinguish it from a single-fixture blockage, and what New Zealand homeowners should do next.
content:
A blocked main sewer drain usually affects several plumbing fixtures at the same time. Toilets may gurgle, showers may drain slowly, wastewater may rise through a floor waste, or an external gully trap may overflow. A blockage affecting only one sink, shower or toilet is more likely to be located close to that fixture.

The distinction matters because a local blockage may be accessible through the fixture trap, while a main drain obstruction sits deeper in the wastewater system. Continuing to use water when the main drain is blocked can force sewage into bathrooms, laundries or outdoor areas.

## What is the main sewer drain?

The main sewer drain carries wastewater away from the property's sanitary fixtures. Toilets, basins, showers, baths, kitchen sinks, dishwashers and washing machines discharge through smaller branch pipes that eventually connect to the main drain or wastewater lateral.

The lateral carries the wastewater towards the public network or, on some properties, an on-site wastewater system. Ownership and maintenance responsibility for laterals vary between New Zealand local authorities. The physical location of a blockage must be established before assuming that it belongs to either the property owner or the council.

## 1. Several fixtures drain slowly

One slow basin usually indicates a local buildup of hair, soap or waste near that basin. A main drain problem is more likely when fixtures in different rooms begin draining slowly together.

Examples include:

- The kitchen sink and laundry tub drain slowly
- A shower and toilet are affected together
- Fixtures on the lowest floor are slower than fixtures upstairs
- Water remains in multiple floor wastes
- Drainage worsens when more than one fixture is used

The shared symptoms occur because wastewater from different branches cannot move freely through the common downstream pipe.

## 2. Toilets gurgle when other fixtures empty

Wastewater moving through a drain also moves air. A restriction can disturb normal airflow and force air through a nearby water trap. This may create bubbling or gurgling in a toilet when a bath empties, a washing machine pumps out or a sink is drained.

An occasional sound is not proof of a main blockage. Persistent gurgling combined with slow drainage, changing toilet water levels or unpleasant smells requires investigation.

Do not repeatedly flush a gurgling toilet to test it. Each flush adds more water to a system that may already be unable to discharge.

## 3. Water rises in a shower or floor waste

Showers and floor wastes often sit lower than other fixtures. When the downstream drain is obstructed, wastewater may return through the lowest available opening.

A common pattern is:

1. A toilet is flushed or washing machine empties.
2. Water appears in a nearby shower or floor waste.
3. The water drains slowly or leaves dirty residue.

This behaviour strongly suggests a shared drain problem. Stop using the affected plumbing until the obstruction has been assessed. Wastewater that appears through a shower or floor waste must be treated as contaminated.

## 4. The external gully trap overflows

A gully trap receives wastewater from fixtures such as sinks, showers and laundries while maintaining a water seal against foul air. It also provides an external overflow point in many drainage layouts.

If the pipe downstream is blocked, wastewater may rise and spill from the gully dish. The overflow may occur only when a fixture is used or may continue if a large volume of water has already entered the system.

Keep roof runoff and surface water away from the gully trap. New Zealand drainage requirements are designed to limit surface water and foreign material entering foul-water systems. A roof downpipe connected to a wastewater gully is a potential cross-connection problem, not a solution for stormwater disposal.

An overflowing gully trap can indicate:

- A blocked private drain or lateral
- Tree-root intrusion
- A damaged or collapsed pipe
- A downstream public-network problem
- An incorrect stormwater connection increasing the flow

Professional [blocked drain unblocking](/blocked-drain-unblocking) may restore flow. If the problem returns, the pipe should be inspected for an underlying defect.

## 5. Sewage smells come from several drains

A dry or dirty trap can cause an odour at one fixture. Smells from several fixtures, combined with slow drainage or gurgling, can indicate retained wastewater in the main drainage system.

Odour alone does not identify the blockage location. Other possible causes include a defective vent, damaged pipe, failed seal or poorly maintained waste fitting. A [drain odour location](/drain-odour-location) service may be appropriate when no visible overflow or simple local cause can be found.

## 6. The blockage returns after plunging

A plunger may move enough waste to restore temporary flow without removing the deeper obstruction. The drain may appear normal for hours or days before slowing again.

Recurring blockages commonly involve:

- Tree roots growing through a failed joint
- Grease and sludge coating the pipe
- A foreign object remaining in the line
- A displaced pipe joint
- A cracked or partially collapsed drain
- A sagging section that retains solids
- An inadequate drain gradient

Repeated plunging does not repair structural damage. A [CCTV drain inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection) can show why the same line keeps blocking.

## 7. Wastewater appears outside

Wet ground, sewage residue or a persistent foul smell above the drain route can indicate leakage or overflow. Wastewater may escape through a gully trap, inspection opening, cracked pipe or damaged joint.

Keep people and animals away from the affected area. Avoid digging until the drain route and other underground services are identified. Excavation without accurate location can create additional damage and safety risks.

## Main drain blockage or single-fixture blockage?

Use the pattern of symptoms as an initial guide.

| Symptom | More likely local blockage | More likely main drain blockage |
|---|---:|---:|
| One basin drains slowly | Yes | No |
| One shower is slow | Yes | Possible |
| One toilet is blocked | Yes | Possible |
| Several fixtures are slow | No | Yes |
| Toilet gurgles when washer empties | No | Yes |
| Water rises through floor waste | No | Yes |
| External gully trap overflows | No | Yes |
| Neighbouring properties also affected | No | Public network possible |

This table is diagnostic guidance, not proof. Pipe layouts differ, and a drain inspection may be required to confirm the location.

## What causes a main sewer drain to block?

### Tree roots

Fine roots enter through cracks and failed joints where moisture escapes. The roots expand inside the pipe and catch paper and waste. Cutting them restores space temporarily, but repairing the pipe defect is necessary to prevent repeated intrusion.

### Wipes and foreign objects

Wet wipes, sanitary products, cloths and other non-dispersible materials can lodge in the drain. They may combine with grease and form a larger obstruction.

### Fat, oil and grease

Kitchen grease cools against pipe walls and traps solid waste. Heavy buildup may require [high-pressure drain cleaning](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning), followed by inspection if the drain has a history of recurring problems.

### Damaged pipework

Cracks, deformation, joint displacement and collapse physically restrict the drain. Cleaning alone cannot correct these defects. The damaged section may require [drain repair](/drainlaying-repairs), replacement or [pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless), depending on its condition and accessibility.

### Poor installation or ground movement

An incorrect gradient, inadequate support or ground settlement can create low points where water and solids remain. Camera footage and level assessment help establish whether the drain needs alteration.

## What to do immediately

### Stop adding water

Do not run taps, flush toilets, shower, use the dishwasher or operate the washing machine. Turning off a tap does not empty the water already stored in appliance cycles or fixtures, so make sure other occupants understand the restriction.

### Keep clear of wastewater

Treat sewage and wastewater as contaminated. Keep children and pets away. Do not use a domestic vacuum on wastewater. Avoid spreading contamination through footwear or cleaning equipment.

### Check the visible symptoms

Without opening or altering pipework, note:

- Which fixtures are affected
- Whether the gully trap is overflowing
- What action triggers the overflow
- Whether neighbouring properties have a similar problem
- Whether the issue has happened before

This information helps determine whether the likely problem is local, private or part of the public network.

### Arrange appropriate help

Drain clearing and restricted drainlaying are not interchangeable. Clearing a blockage may restore flow, while repairs or alterations to sanitary drainage require a person authorised for the relevant work in New Zealand.

Use [emergency plumbing assistance](/emergency-plumbing) when wastewater is actively entering the home, toilets cannot be used, or continued overflow is causing property damage.

## How the blockage is located

A systematic investigation may include:

1. Checking affected fixtures and the gully trap
2. Identifying accessible inspection points
3. Clearing enough of the obstruction to restore access
4. Cleaning the pipe with suitable mechanical or water-jetting equipment
5. Inspecting the line with a CCTV camera
6. Locating defects from the surface
7. Recommending cleaning, repair, relining or replacement

A camera placed into a drain that remains completely blocked may provide limited visibility. Initial clearance is sometimes needed before a meaningful inspection can be completed.

## Who pays for the blockage?

There is no single nationwide boundary rule that applies identically to every property. Local authorities define responsibility for private laterals and public wastewater assets differently.

As a practical sequence:

1. Establish whether the blockage is inside the property drainage or lateral.
2. Record the obstruction and any pipe defect.
3. Check the relevant council or water authority's lateral policy.
4. Contact the authority if evidence indicates a public-main problem.

Several neighbouring homes losing drainage together, or wastewater emerging from a public manhole, increases the likelihood of a network issue. A blockage confined to one property's fixtures or private gully trap is more likely to require private investigation first.

## Can chemical drain cleaner clear a main sewer blockage?

Chemical cleaner is not a practical answer to tree roots, collapsed pipework, heavy grease deposits or lodged objects in a main drain. The chemical may remain trapped in wastewater and expose whoever later opens or clears the line.

Never mix drain-cleaning products. Tell the drainage professional if any chemical has already been poured into the system.

## Preventing another main drain blockage

- Flush only human waste and toilet paper
- Keep wipes and sanitary products out of toilets
- Keep cooking fats and food scraps out of sinks
- Maintain outdoor grates and stormwater inlets
- Investigate recurring gurgling and slow drainage early
- Obtain a CCTV inspection after root removal or repeated blockage
- Repair cracked, displaced or collapsed sections
- Confirm stormwater and wastewater pipes are correctly separated
- Use an authorised drainlayer when restricted drainage work is required

## Frequently asked questions

### Can a main sewer drain be blocked if only one toilet is affected?

Yes, but a local toilet blockage is more likely when every other fixture drains normally. The risk of a main blockage increases when the toilet problem occurs with shower backup, gurgling or an overflowing gully trap.

### Why does the shower fill when the toilet is flushed?

The shower may be the lowest opening connected upstream of a shared blockage. Water from the toilet cannot travel downstream, so it returns through the shower waste.

### Is an overflowing gully trap always a council problem?

No. It often indicates a blockage in private drainage or a private wastewater lateral. Responsibility depends on the blockage location and the local authority's asset boundary.

### Will hydro jetting repair a cracked drain?

No. Hydro jetting removes deposits and some obstructions. It does not seal cracks, reconnect displaced joints or rebuild a collapsed pipe. Damaged drains require a repair method suited to their condition.

### When is CCTV inspection necessary?

CCTV inspection is useful after recurring blockages, root removal, suspected structural damage, unexplained outdoor leakage or before deciding between excavation and relining.

### Should water use stop if the drain is only slow?

Reduce water use and arrange assessment if several fixtures are slow. Stop using the system completely if wastewater begins backing up or overflowing.

title: Blocked Drain vs Blocked Toilet: How to Identify the Problem
excerpt: Compare the signs of a blocked toilet and a blocked main drain. Learn what each symptom means, what to avoid, and when professional drain help is required.
content:
A blocked toilet usually affects one toilet. A blocked main drain can affect toilets, showers, basins, floor wastes and external gully traps together. Checking which fixtures respond when water is used helps distinguish a local toilet obstruction from a deeper wastewater drainage problem.

Do not keep flushing to test a toilet that is already full. One additional flush can spill contaminated water across the floor. If wastewater is returning through another fixture or an external gully trap, stop using the property's plumbing until the blockage is assessed.

## Quick comparison

| Observation | Blocked toilet more likely | Main drain blockage more likely |
|---|---:|---:|
| Only one toilet is affected | Yes | Possible |
| Other toilets work normally | Yes | Less likely |
| Toilet bowl fills but slowly falls | Yes | Possible |
| Shower backs up when toilet flushes | No | Yes |
| Several fixtures drain slowly | No | Yes |
| External gully trap overflows | No | Yes |
| Toilet gurgles when washing machine empties | No | Yes |
| Wastewater appears at lowest floor waste | No | Yes |

The symptoms indicate probability, not certainty. Plumbing layouts vary, and the blockage location may require testing or camera inspection.

## Signs of a blockage inside or near the toilet

### Only one toilet is affected

If one toilet does not drain but sinks, showers and other toilets work normally, the obstruction is probably in the toilet trap or nearby branch pipe.

Common causes include:

- Excessive toilet paper
- Wet wipes
- Sanitary products
- A small toy or household object
- A toilet-cleaning accessory
- An incorrectly fitted or damaged pan connector

The toilet's internal trap is designed to retain water and block sewer gases. Its curved shape can also catch objects that should not have been flushed.

### The bowl rises after flushing

Water that rises toward the rim shows that discharge is not leaving at the expected rate. If the water then falls slowly, a partial obstruction may remain. If it stays high, the passage may be completely blocked.

Remove the cistern lid only if it can be done safely and without disturbing components. Prevent another flush by telling other occupants not to use the toilet. Do not pour additional buckets of water into a full bowl.

### The blockage followed a known event

A local blockage is more likely when the toilet stopped working immediately after excessive paper or an object was flushed. That does not guarantee the item is still inside the toilet; it may have moved into the branch drain.

Tell the person clearing the blockage what entered the toilet. Knowing whether the obstruction is paper, cloth, plastic or a solid object affects the safest removal method.

## Signs of a blocked main drain

### Other fixtures react to a toilet flush

Flush water should travel away without changing the water level in a shower or floor waste. If water rises elsewhere, the shared downstream drain is restricted.

The same principle applies when:

- A toilet bubbles while the bath empties
- A shower gurgles when the basin drains
- The laundry tub fills when the washing machine pumps out
- A floor waste overflows during dishwasher use

These cross-fixture symptoms are stronger evidence of a [blocked main drain](/blocked-drain-unblocking) than the behaviour of one toilet alone.

### Several toilets are affected

Two toilets blocking at the same time are unlikely to have separate local obstructions, particularly if they sit on the same level. The shared branch or main drain should be investigated.

In a multi-level building, the lowest fixtures may show symptoms first because wastewater returns through the lowest available opening.

### The external gully trap overflows

An external gully trap receives wastewater from selected household fixtures and maintains a water seal against foul air. Wastewater rising from it often indicates a downstream obstruction.

Do not cover or seal the gully opening to stop the overflow. The water may then find a lower opening inside the building. Keep surface runoff, soil, leaves and roof water away from the gully.

### Sewage odour affects several areas

An odour at one basin may come from its trap or waste fitting. Smells across several fixtures may accompany retained wastewater, a drainage defect or a ventilation problem. Odour combined with slow flow and gurgling strengthens the case for a shared drain issue.

## Why toilets become blocked

### Too much paper at once

Toilet paper breaks apart in water, but a large compacted quantity can temporarily obstruct a narrow passage. Repeated flushing adds pressure and volume without guaranteeing that the paper disperses.

### Wet wipes

Wet wipes remain stronger in water than toilet paper. Products described as flushable can still catch on joints, roots and rough pipe surfaces. Wipes should be placed in household rubbish rather than the toilet.

### Sanitary products and nappies

These products absorb liquid and expand. They can lodge in a toilet trap or farther along the drain. They should never enter the wastewater system through a toilet.

### Foreign objects

Toys, air freshener holders, cleaning cloths and plastic items may disappear from the bowl but remain trapped out of sight. A solid object may require removal rather than being forced farther into the pipe.

### An existing drainage defect

A toilet may appear to be the problem because it is the first fixture to show symptoms. Roots, a collapsed pipe or a poor drain gradient farther downstream can repeatedly restrict toilet discharge.

## Why the toilet keeps blocking

A toilet that blocks once after excessive paper may have a simple cause. A toilet that blocks repeatedly under normal use indicates an unresolved problem.

Possible causes include:

- An object lodged in the toilet trap
- A restricted branch drain
- Weak flushing caused by a cistern or rim-flow problem
- Incorrect toilet installation
- A damaged pan connector
- Tree roots in the main drain
- A cracked or displaced pipe
- A sagging drainage section
- Heavy buildup inside the wastewater line

Repeated plunging may restore temporary flow while leaving the underlying fault untouched. If the same toilet continues blocking, the next step should be diagnosis, not stronger force.

## Safe initial checks

### Check other fixtures

Without discharging large amounts of water, observe whether nearby basins, showers and other toilets are also slow. Do not run appliances through full cycles merely to test the drainage.

### Look at the external gully trap

Check for a high water level or overflow. Do not put hands, tools or chemicals into contaminated wastewater. An overflowing gully trap points towards a downstream issue.

### Ask what was flushed

Direct information can save time. Children or other occupants may know whether a toy, wipe or excessive paper entered the toilet.

### Stop water use if several fixtures are affected

Continuing to use taps and appliances can turn a partial blockage into an indoor sewage overflow.

## Can a plunger clear the toilet?

A correctly used toilet plunger can clear some soft local obstructions. The bowl needs enough water to cover the plunger cup, but it should not be close to overflowing. Use steady controlled pressure rather than violent splashing.

Stop if:

- The bowl is about to overflow
- A solid object may be trapped
- Chemical cleaner has been added
- Water rises in another fixture
- The gully trap is overflowing
- The toilet or its connection appears damaged

A plunger cannot repair roots, collapsed drains, displaced joints or an incorrectly installed toilet.

## Why chemical drain cleaners are a poor toilet solution

Chemical products may remain in the toilet bowl when the line is fully blocked. This creates a splash and contact hazard during later mechanical clearing. Some products can damage seals or react dangerously when combined with another cleaner.

Never mix chemicals. If a product has already been used, tell the person attending the blockage before work begins.

## When the toilet may need removal

Removing a toilet may be necessary when a solid object is lodged in its trap, the pan connector is defective or access through the fixture is required. Toilet removal and reinstatement must protect the connection, seals and surrounding surfaces.

A cracked pan, unstable fixture or leaking connection may require [toilet repair or replacement](/toilet-installation-repair) rather than blockage clearance alone.

## When drain cleaning is needed

Drain-cleaning equipment is more appropriate when the obstruction lies beyond the toilet or affects several fixtures. The method depends on the likely material and pipe condition.

- Mechanical equipment can retrieve or break through certain obstructions.
- Cutting heads can remove roots, although the entry defect remains.
- [High-pressure water jetting](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) can remove grease, sludge and loose deposits.
- [CCTV drain inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection) can identify roots, broken pipes and retained objects.

The line may need initial clearing before a camera can obtain usable footage.

## Blockage clearance vs drainage repair

Clearing removes an obstruction and restores flow. Repair corrects a physical defect in the drainage system.

A drain may require repair when inspection finds:

- Cracks or holes
- Displaced joints
- Deformation or collapse
- Root-entry points
- Incorrect gradient
- A persistent low section

Repair options may include local excavation, pipe replacement or [trenchless pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless). Relining is not suitable for every condition; a severely collapsed or incorrectly aligned drain may need excavation.

## Who should handle the problem?

A minor accessible toilet obstruction and restricted drainlaying work are different activities. New Zealand regulates sanitary plumbing and drainlaying. Altering, repairing or replacing relevant pipework requires appropriate authorisation for the trade involved.

Use urgent assistance when:

- Wastewater is entering occupied rooms
- The only toilet cannot be used
- Multiple toilets are blocked
- A gully trap is overflowing continuously
- Commercial premises cannot operate safely
- Contaminated water is spreading across the property

An [emergency plumber](/emergency-plumbing) may control the immediate problem, while an authorised drainlayer may be required if underground sanitary drainage needs repair.

## Preventing toilet and drain blockages

- Flush only human waste and toilet paper
- Put wipes in rubbish, even when their packaging says flushable
- Keep sanitary products, nappies and cotton items out of toilets
- Use a child-safe lid arrangement where toys are a risk
- Do not dispose of cooking grease through sinks
- Act on recurring gurgling or slow drainage
- Inspect drains after repeated root blockage
- Repair structural defects instead of repeatedly clearing them

## Frequently asked questions

### Why is my toilet blocked but the sink still works?

The obstruction may be inside the toilet trap or its branch connection. A working sink makes a main drain blockage less likely but does not completely exclude it, especially if the sink joins the system downstream.

### Why does water come up in the shower when I flush?

The shared drain is probably restricted downstream of both fixtures. Toilet discharge returns through the lower shower waste because it cannot travel normally toward the wastewater network.

### Can repeated flushing clear a toilet?

Repeated flushing is more likely to overflow the bowl than solve a developed obstruction. Stop after the first abnormal rise in water level.

### Is a gurgling toilet definitely blocked?

No. Gurgling can also involve drainage ventilation or fixture issues. Gurgling accompanied by slow drains, changing water levels or gully overflow strongly suggests a restriction.

### Should a blocked toilet be treated as an emergency?

It is urgent when sewage is overflowing, several fixtures are affected, the property has no usable toilet, or contaminated water threatens occupied areas. A single blocked toilet is less urgent when another safe toilet remains available and no wastewater is escaping.

### Why does the toilet block again after plunging?

The plunger may have moved only part of the obstruction. A retained object, pipe defect, roots or weak flush performance can cause the same problem to return.

title: CCTV Drain Inspection: When Is It Actually Needed?
excerpt: Learn when a CCTV drain inspection is useful, what the camera can find, what it cannot do, and how footage supports drain repair decisions.
content:
A CCTV drain inspection is needed when the cause or condition of a drain cannot be established from the surface. It is especially useful for recurring blockages, tree-root intrusion, suspected pipe damage, unexplained wastewater leakage and pre-repair assessment. A camera records the inside of the pipe so cleaning and repair decisions can be based on visible evidence.

A drain camera does not clear a blockage or repair a pipe. The line may need cleaning before the camera can travel through it and produce useful footage.

## What is a CCTV drain inspection?

A CCTV inspection uses a waterproof camera attached to a flexible cable or specialised crawler. The camera enters the drainage system through a suitable access point and transmits video as it moves along the pipe.

The operator records observations such as:

- Pipe material and approximate diameter
- Joint condition
- Cracks and holes
- Root intrusion
- Deformation or collapse
- Accumulated grease or debris
- Standing water
- Displaced connections
- Foreign objects
- The approximate location of defects

Some camera systems include a transmitter that can be located from the surface. This allows a defect to be marked before excavation or repair planning.

## 1. The same drain keeps blocking

A one-off blockage may be caused by waste that entered the system during normal use. Repeated blockage in the same line suggests that something inside the drain continues to catch or retain material.

Possible recurring causes include:

- Roots growing through a failed joint
- A cracked section
- A displaced connection
- A sagging pipe that holds water and solids
- Grease remaining on the pipe wall
- An object that was never fully removed
- Partial pipe collapse
- Inadequate gradient

Repeated [blocked drain unblocking](/blocked-drain-unblocking) without inspection can become an expensive cycle. Camera footage helps separate a maintenance problem from a structural defect.

## 2. Tree roots have been removed

Root cutting restores space inside the drain but does not close the crack or joint through which the roots entered. Inspection after root removal can reveal:

- The entry point
- The condition of the surrounding pipe
- Whether several joints are affected
- How much root material remains
- Whether repair, replacement or monitoring is appropriate

Without this information, the roots may simply grow back. A suitable section may qualify for [pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless), while a collapsed or badly displaced drain may require excavation.

## 3. A drain is slow but not completely blocked

Persistent slow flow can indicate buildup, damage or poor alignment. A camera may show standing water, deposits or a physical restriction. The pipe should normally be cleaned enough for the camera lens and cable to move safely.

Camera inspection is less informative when the lens is submerged in dirty water or pressed against a complete obstruction. The correct sequence may be initial clearing, pipe cleaning and then inspection.

## 4. Wastewater is leaking or appearing outside

An unexplained wet patch, sewage smell or wastewater emerging from the ground can indicate a cracked pipe, defective joint or overflowing drainage point. Camera footage can identify visible internal defects and help narrow the excavation area.

Not every leak is visible from inside the pipe. Additional testing or surface location may be required when footage looks normal but evidence of leakage remains.

## 5. A property is being purchased

A standard building inspection may not include a complete internal survey of underground wastewater drains. A separate CCTV drain inspection can be useful when:

- The property is older
- Large trees grow near the drain route
- Drainage repairs have been reported
- Gully traps show overflow residue
- Ground settlement is visible
- The buyer plans extensions or major renovation
- The property has a history of blockages

The inspection can reveal defects that are not visible during a normal walk-through. It does not guarantee the future performance of every drain and should be interpreted with the property's layout, age and maintenance history.

Link the inspection with a wider [pre-purchase plumbing inspection](/pre-purchase-plumbing-inspection) when the condition of water supply, hot-water equipment and internal fixtures also needs review.

## 6. A repair method must be selected

Drain repair should match the actual defect. CCTV footage helps determine whether the problem is local or widespread and whether the pipe retains enough shape for a trenchless option.

Possible outcomes include:

- Cleaning only
- Localised excavation and repair
- Replacement of a longer section
- Junction repair
- Root removal followed by monitoring
- Structural relining
- No immediate repair, with a future inspection schedule

Relining is not an automatic answer to every damaged drain. A fully collapsed pipe, severe displacement or incorrect gradient may require [drain excavation and replacement](/drainlaying-repairs).

## 7. Building or renovation work is planned

Existing drains may cross a proposed foundation, extension or excavation area. Inspection and location can help identify the route and condition of the line before work begins.

Camera footage alone may not provide every detail required for design or consent documentation. Drain plans, surface locating, levels and input from an authorised drainlayer may also be necessary.

## 8. Workmanship or damage needs to be documented

Pre-work and post-work footage can document pipe condition. It may be useful when underground construction, utility work or drain cleaning takes place near an existing line.

The video should be accompanied by clear identification of the property, access point, inspection direction and defect location. Unlabelled footage with no distance or site context has limited value.

## What can a drain camera detect?

### Tree roots

Roots appear as fine strands, dense masses or larger growth entering through joints and cracks. Heavy roots may stop the camera until cutting is completed.

### Cracks and fractures

The camera may show longitudinal cracks, circumferential fractures, missing pieces or holes. Visibility depends on pipe cleanliness, lighting and camera position.

### Displaced joints

A joint may separate or move out of alignment, creating an edge that catches waste. Soil or roots may enter through the gap.

### Pipe deformation and collapse

Plastic pipes can deform under loading or poor bedding. Other pipe materials may fracture and lose shape. A camera can show restriction but may be unable to pass through a complete collapse.

### Grease and debris

Deposits appear along the invert or pipe wall. Heavy contamination may need [hydro jetting](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) before the structural surface can be assessed.

### Standing water

Water remaining in a drain can indicate a low point, adverse gradient, downstream restriction or temporary flow condition. Levels and repeat observations may be required to interpret it correctly.

### Foreign objects

The camera may locate wipes, cloth, construction debris, broken fittings or other objects. Retrieval method depends on the object's shape, location and access.

## What a CCTV inspection cannot prove by itself

A camera is a visual tool, not a complete engineering investigation. It may not establish:

- The exact depth without compatible locating equipment
- The condition of soil around the pipe
- A small leak that is not visually apparent
- The cause of every area of standing water
- The remaining service life of the entire system
- Ownership of the pipe
- Whether council approval or building consent is required
- The condition of a branch the camera did not enter

Footage should be interpreted by someone who understands drainage layouts, materials and repair methods.

## Does the drain need cleaning first?

Often, yes. Grease, silt and wastewater can hide cracks and joints. A camera pushed through a dirty pipe may record the deposits rather than the pipe condition.

A practical sequence is:

1. Confirm the affected fixtures and access points.
2. Remove the immediate blockage.
3. Clean the drain where necessary.
4. Inspect the internal surface.
5. Locate and record defects.
6. Recommend the appropriate next action.

Post-cleaning footage can also confirm whether the obstruction has been removed.

## How a CCTV drain inspection is performed

### Access is selected

The camera may enter through a purpose-made inspection point, an accessible drain opening or another suitable location. A toilet or fitting should not be removed unnecessarily when safe access already exists.

### The camera is advanced through the pipe

The operator moves the camera slowly enough to inspect joints and defects. Distance information may be recorded, but it must be related to a known starting point.

### Defects are located

When the system supports surface locating, the camera head or transmitter can be traced above ground. The location and approximate depth are marked for repair planning.

### Findings are recorded

Useful deliverables may include video, still images, defect notes, approximate distances and a repair recommendation. Ask what documentation is included before booking if the footage is required for a purchase, dispute or construction project.

## Questions to ask before booking

- Is blockage clearing included or charged separately?
- Will the drain be cleaned before inspection if necessary?
- Will I receive the video or a written report?
- Can defects be located from the surface?
- Does the quote cover one line or the full accessible drainage system?
- What happens if the camera cannot pass the obstruction?
- Can the provider carry out authorised drain repairs if damage is found?

The cheapest camera pass may not provide a useful diagnosis if it excludes cleaning, locating and reporting.

## CCTV inspection before pipe relining

Relining places a new structural or repair liner inside an existing pipe. Inspection is required to assess whether the drain is a suitable candidate and to measure the affected section.

The process may require:

- Cleaning and root removal
- Pre-lining CCTV footage
- Measurement of pipe diameter and length
- Identification of bends and junctions
- Confirmation that the pipe has not fully collapsed
- Post-lining inspection

A liner should not conceal an unresolved alignment problem or inappropriate connection.

## CCTV inspection and property responsibility

Camera footage can help identify whether a defect lies within a private drain, private lateral or area that may involve a public asset. It does not determine legal responsibility on its own.

New Zealand councils and water authorities use different lateral ownership boundaries. Confirm the relevant local policy after the defect location has been established. Retain the footage and written findings if a council or third party needs evidence.

## When CCTV inspection may not be necessary

A full camera inspection may add little value when:

- A simple obstruction is visible and fully removed from an accessible trap
- The drain has no history of slow flow or repeated blockage
- The affected fitting itself is clearly defective
- The line is known to require complete replacement for unrelated reasons

Inspection becomes more valuable when symptoms recur, the drain is buried, repair costs are significant or the cause remains uncertain.

## Frequently asked questions

### Can a drain camera see through standing water?

Visibility is limited when the lens is submerged in dirty water. The drain may need clearing or cleaning before its condition can be assessed properly.

### Does CCTV inspection include drain cleaning?

Not automatically. Inspection and cleaning are separate services unless the quote combines them. Confirm what is included before work starts.

### Can CCTV find a collapsed drain?

It can show deformation or the point where the camera cannot continue. Surface locating may then mark the obstruction for excavation. The camera cannot travel beyond a complete collapse.

### Is CCTV useful after hydro jetting?

Yes. Post-cleaning footage can confirm that deposits were removed and reveal cracks, roots or joint defects previously hidden by debris.

### Should drains be inspected before buying a house?

Inspection is worth considering for older properties, sites with large trees, known drainage history, visible overflow evidence or planned building work. It is not automatically necessary for every transaction.

### Can camera footage show who is responsible for repair?

It can show the defect and help locate it. Responsibility then depends on property boundaries, pipe ownership and the relevant council or water authority's policy.

title: Hydro Jetting vs Traditional Drain Cleaning: Which Method Is Better?
excerpt: Compare hydro jetting with mechanical drain cleaning. Learn which method suits grease, roots, recurring blockages and damaged drains.
content:
Hydro jetting cleans drains with controlled high-pressure water. Traditional mechanical cleaning uses rotating cables, cutting heads or retrieval tools to break through or remove an obstruction. Hydro jetting is generally stronger for grease, sludge and widespread deposits. Mechanical equipment may be better for solid objects, initial access and selected root blockages.

Neither method repairs a cracked, displaced or collapsed drain. The pipe's condition, material, diameter and type of blockage should determine the equipment used.

## What is hydro jetting?

Hydro jetting sends water through a specialised hose and nozzle. Rear-facing jets help move the hose through the pipe and wash loosened material downstream. Forward-facing or purpose-specific jets may be used to penetrate deposits, clean pipe walls or cut certain root growth.

The equipment must be selected and operated for the drainage system being cleaned. Maximum pressure alone does not define an effective job. Water flow, nozzle design, hose size, access direction and operator control all affect the result.

[Hydro jetting and high-pressure drain cleaning](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) can remove:

- Fat, oil and grease
- Soap and organic sludge
- Silt and loose sediment
- Scale and surface deposits
- Paper and soft waste
- Some fine tree roots
- Residue left after an obstruction is opened

## What is traditional mechanical drain cleaning?

Mechanical cleaning places a flexible cable, rod or rotating tool into the drain. Different heads can pierce a soft obstruction, cut roots, scrape deposits or retrieve foreign material.

Mechanical methods are useful when:

- A complete blockage prevents initial water flow
- A solid object must be retrieved
- Dense roots require cutting
- Water use must be limited
- Access is narrow or difficult
- The pipe needs to be opened before camera inspection

A small hole through a blockage may restore temporary drainage without cleaning the entire pipe wall. That is why a drain can block again after a basic cable pass.

## Key differences

| Factor | Hydro jetting | Mechanical cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| Main cleaning force | High-pressure water | Cable, rod or cutting tool |
| Grease removal | Strong | Limited to moderate |
| Pipe-wall cleaning | Broad | Usually localised |
| Solid-object retrieval | Poor | Often better |
| Root cutting | Tool-dependent | Strong with suitable cutter |
| Water requirement | High | Low |
| Damaged-pipe risk | Requires assessment | Requires assessment |
| Post-cleaning CCTV | Often useful | Often useful |

The methods are not direct competitors in every case. A drainage professional may use mechanical equipment to open a line, hydro jetting to clean it and CCTV to check the result.

## When hydro jetting is the better option

### Grease and fat buildup

Kitchen grease sticks to the pipe wall and catches food particles. A cable may create a channel through the centre while leaving a thick coating behind. Water jetting can wash a broader area and carry loosened material out of the line.

Commercial kitchens and food premises may develop heavier deposits because of higher wastewater volumes. Grease management and appropriate [grease-trap servicing](/grease-trap-cleaning) remain necessary; jetting the downstream drain does not correct poor waste practices or an ineffective trap.

### Recurring soft blockages

A line that repeatedly blocks with paper, soap or organic sludge may contain residual deposits. High-pressure cleaning can remove material that a basic opening pass leaves behind.

Recurring blockage still requires investigation. Deposits may be collecting because of a sagging pipe, displaced joint or incorrect gradient. Cleaning alone will not correct those defects.

### Silt and loose sediment

Stormwater lines can collect sand, leaves and silt. Suitable water jetting can mobilise loose deposits where there is a safe discharge route. The entry point should also be corrected, or the sediment will return.

### Preparing a drain for CCTV inspection

A dirty pipe can hide cracks and joints from the camera. Cleaning before [CCTV drain inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection) gives a clearer view of the structural surface.

### Cleaning after root cutting

A cutter may remove the main root mass while leaving fine material and trapped waste behind. Jetting can clean the line before camera inspection. The cracked joint or hole that admitted the roots must still be repaired if long-term prevention is required.

## When mechanical cleaning may be better

### A solid object is trapped

Toys, cloth, plastic and broken fittings should not automatically be pushed farther downstream. Retrieval tools or controlled mechanical access may be more appropriate than water pressure.

### The drain is completely blocked

Jetting into a completely blocked line can cause water to return through the access point or another fixture. Mechanical equipment may first create a path and identify the material.

### Dense tree roots

Heavy roots may require a cutting head. The drain should then be inspected to locate the entry point and assess whether [pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless) or excavation is appropriate.

### The pipe condition is uncertain

An old or damaged drain may not be suitable for aggressive cleaning. Initial camera inspection may be possible from the accessible side, or a more conservative method may be chosen until the pipe condition is understood.

## Can hydro jetting damage drains?

Incorrect equipment or technique can damage vulnerable pipework, fittings and seals. Risk increases when a drain is already cracked, badly corroded, deformed or partially collapsed.

Before high-pressure cleaning, the operator should consider:

- Pipe material and estimated age
- Diameter and layout
- Known defects
- Access point condition
- Discharge direction
- Nearby fixtures and connections
- Whether wastewater can safely leave the line

Pressure should be matched to the task. Using the highest available setting is not a measure of quality.

## Does hydro jetting remove tree roots permanently?

No. Cutting or jetting removes roots inside the drain. Roots entered because the pipe had a crack, failed joint or other opening. They can grow back while that defect remains.

A long-term response involves:

1. Removing the root obstruction
2. Cleaning the drain
3. Inspecting with CCTV
4. Locating the entry point
5. Repairing, replacing or relining the defective section

Periodic cleaning may be used when immediate repair is not practical, but it is maintenance rather than a permanent fix.

## Does hydro jetting fix a collapsed pipe?

No. Water cannot restore a pipe that has lost its shape, separated or collapsed. Attempting to force equipment through a structural failure can worsen the damage or trap the hose.

Structural problems require [drain repair or replacement](/drainlaying-repairs). CCTV footage and surface location help determine the repair area.

## Hydro jetting for stormwater drains

Stormwater drains carry rainwater rather than household wastewater. They may block with leaves, roof debris, soil, stones and silt. Jetting may clean accessible pipework, but the outlet must be identified and protected from uncontrolled discharge.

A stormwater blockage can also involve a crushed pipe, root intrusion, inadequate capacity or an outlet problem. Cleaning will not correct a system that is undersized or incorrectly connected.

Stormwater must remain separate from the foul-water system. Suspected cross-connections need proper investigation and authorised correction.

## Hydro jetting for commercial drains

Commercial drainage can involve longer runs, larger diameters and waste specific to the business. Restaurants may generate fat and food residue. Workshops may produce sediment. Shared facilities may experience wipes and excessive paper.

The cleaning plan should account for:

- Business operating hours
- Food-safety requirements
- Wastewater containment
- Grease-trap condition
- Access and traffic control
- Pipe plans and shared connections
- Documentation of pre-existing defects

Preventive cleaning should be based on actual deposit rates, not an arbitrary schedule copied across every site.

## How the correct method is selected

### Step 1: Identify the symptoms

One slow fixture suggests a local problem. Several affected fixtures, gurgling and gully overflow suggest a shared drain blockage.

### Step 2: Establish access and flow

The drainage professional identifies safe access and determines whether any water can pass the obstruction.

### Step 3: Consider the likely material

Grease, roots, silt and solid objects require different tools. Information about what entered the drain is valuable.

### Step 4: Clear and clean

Mechanical opening, retrieval, root cutting or water jetting is performed as appropriate.

### Step 5: Inspect recurring problems

CCTV footage confirms whether the drain is clean and identifies structural defects.

## Signs that cleaning alone is not enough

- The blockage returns within a short period
- Roots repeatedly enter the same location
- CCTV shows cracks or displaced joints
- Water remains in a low section after cleaning
- The pipe is deformed or collapsed
- Wastewater leaks into surrounding ground
- Surface settlement appears above the drain
- The drain has an incorrect gradient

These conditions require a repair decision, not another routine cleaning visit.

## Questions to ask before booking drain cleaning

- What method is proposed and why?
- Is CCTV inspection included?
- Will the pipe be inspected after cleaning?
- What happens if structural damage is found?
- Is wastewater and debris removal included?
- Can defects be located from the surface?
- Will video or findings be provided?
- Is the provider authorised for any restricted drain repair that may follow?

## Frequently asked questions

### Is hydro jetting better than drain snaking?

It is better for broad pipe-wall cleaning and many grease or sludge deposits. Mechanical cleaning is often better for initial penetration, retrieval and selected root cutting. The obstruction and pipe condition decide the method.

### How long does hydro jetting keep a drain clear?

There is no fixed period. A sound drain kept free of unsuitable waste may remain clear. A cracked, sagging or root-damaged drain can block again until its defect is repaired.

### Can hydro jetting be used in old pipes?

Possibly, but the pipe condition must be assessed. Fragile, corroded or damaged drains require cautious equipment selection and may need inspection before cleaning.

### Does hydro jetting use chemicals?

The primary cleaning force is water. The service scope should state if any separate chemical or treatment product is proposed.

### Should CCTV be completed after hydro jetting?

It is valuable when blockages recur, roots were present or structural damage is suspected. Post-cleaning footage shows the pipe surface more clearly.

### Can homeowners hire a water blaster and jet the drain?

Domestic equipment and improvised hoses can become trapped, damage fittings or spread contaminated wastewater. Underground drainage may contain structural defects and restricted work must be handled by an appropriately authorised person.

title: How Tree Roots Enter and Damage Underground Drains
excerpt: Learn how tree roots enter underground drains, the warning signs they cause, how roots are removed, and why damaged pipes often need repair.
content:
Tree roots enter drains through existing cracks, failed joints and damaged connections. Moisture and nutrients escaping from the pipe encourage fine roots to grow through the opening. The roots expand inside the drain, catch wastewater solids and eventually restrict or block the pipe.

Removing the roots restores flow but does not close the defect. A lasting solution usually requires inspection and repair of the opening that allowed root entry.

## Do tree roots break into sound drains?

Roots follow moisture through surrounding soil. A correctly installed, structurally sound and watertight drain provides no easy entry path. Intrusion normally begins where the drainage system already has a weakness, such as:

- A cracked pipe wall
- A separated or displaced joint
- A deteriorated seal
- A poorly formed connection
- A damaged inspection point
- An unused connection that was not sealed correctly
- A section disturbed by ground movement

Very small roots can enter a narrow opening. Once inside, the continuous supply of water supports further growth.

## Why underground drains attract roots

Wastewater pipes can release moisture, warmth and nutrients through defects. Roots naturally extend toward favourable soil conditions. The drain does not need to leak heavily; persistent dampness near a failed joint can be enough.

The risk depends on more than the size of the tree. Drain condition, distance, soil moisture, pipe material and root behaviour all matter. Removing a nearby tree does not automatically remove roots already inside the drainage system.

## Signs of tree-root intrusion

### Recurring blockages

The strongest warning is a blockage that returns after previous clearing. Cutting creates space, but new growth enters through the same defect.

### Several slow fixtures

Roots in a main drain restrict wastewater from multiple branches. Toilets, showers and sinks may slow together.

### Gurgling toilets

Restricted wastewater and airflow can cause bubbling or gurgling through toilet traps, especially when another fixture empties.

### External gully overflow

Wastewater may rise from the gully trap when the downstream drain is obstructed. Stop household water use if an overflow begins.

### Sewage smells or wet ground

A damaged drain can leak into surrounding soil. Persistent odour, unusual dampness or lush growth over the pipe route requires investigation.

### A history of mature trees near older drains

Trees alone do not prove intrusion, but mature vegetation near an older line increases suspicion when drainage symptoms recur.

## How roots create a blockage

Fine roots first appear as strands through a joint or crack. Paper, wipes, grease and solids catch on the strands. The combined mass reduces flow and traps more waste.

As growth continues, roots can:

- Fill a large part of the pipe
- Widen an existing opening
- Displace a weakened joint
- Make cleaning equipment difficult to pass
- Contribute to leakage and soil erosion
- Hide the structural condition during camera inspection

The root mass is both an obstruction and evidence of a pipe defect.

## How root intrusion is confirmed

### Initial drain clearing

A complete blockage may need to be opened before inspection. Mechanical cutting or another suitable method restores enough flow and camera access.

### CCTV drain inspection

A [CCTV drain camera](/cctv-drain-inspection) can show roots entering through joints, fractures and connections. The operator should record the distance from a known access point.

### Surface location

A compatible transmitter can help mark the defect above ground. Accurate location limits unnecessary excavation.

### Post-cleaning inspection

Roots and waste can hide cracks. The pipe should be cleaned before its final condition is assessed.

## Methods used to remove tree roots

### Mechanical root cutting

A rotating cutting head removes the root mass and restores an opening. Tool size must suit the pipe diameter and condition. Cutting too aggressively in a damaged drain can worsen the defect.

### High-pressure water cleaning

Purpose-specific jetting equipment may remove fine roots and flush loose debris. Dense woody growth may require mechanical cutting first. [Hydro jetting](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) is also useful for cleaning the pipe before camera inspection.

### Excavation

When the drain is collapsed, badly displaced or inaccessible, excavation may be required to remove the affected section and rebuild the connection.

Root-killing chemicals are not a substitute for structural repair. They may affect vegetation, wastewater treatment and worker safety, and they do not seal the entry point.

## Why root cutting is usually temporary

Cutting treats what is inside the pipe. The root source and pipe opening remain. New growth can return through the same path.

The recurrence rate cannot be predicted from root cutting alone. It depends on the tree, defect size, moisture, season and effectiveness of removal. Claims that one cleaning will permanently stop roots are not credible unless the entry defect is also sealed or replaced.

## Repair options after root removal

### Local excavation and repair

A short defective section can be exposed, removed and replaced. Excavation provides direct access and allows correction of alignment, bedding and connections.

It may disrupt paving, gardens or driveways, but it is often necessary when the pipe has collapsed or shifted significantly.

### Pipe relining

[Trenchless pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless) installs a liner within the existing pipe. A suitable liner can seal cracks and joints while creating a new internal surface.

Relining requires:

- Adequate remaining pipe shape
- Thorough cleaning and root removal
- Correct measurement
- Suitable access
- Proper treatment of junctions
- Pre- and post-installation CCTV inspection

A fully collapsed pipe cannot simply be lined without restoring a path and acceptable geometry.

### Full drain replacement

Replacement may be appropriate when defects affect a long section, gradient is wrong, several connections have failed or the pipe material is extensively deteriorated.

An authorised drainlayer should assess restricted sanitary-drainage repair in New Zealand.

## Relining vs excavation for root-damaged drains

| Condition | Relining may suit | Excavation may suit |
|---|---:|---:|
| Cracked but aligned pipe | Yes | Yes |
| Roots through several joints | Yes | Possible |
| Pipe fully collapsed | No | Yes |
| Major joint displacement | Limited | Yes |
| Incorrect gradient | No | Yes |
| Access under paving | Often | Disruptive |
| Connection needs rebuilding | Limited | Yes |

The lowest-disruption method is not automatically the correct method. Repair must correct the actual defect.

## Should the tree be removed?

Tree removal is a separate arboricultural decision. Removing a healthy tree may not be necessary when the drain can be repaired and sealed. It also does not remove dead roots immediately or restore damaged pipework.

Consider:

- Tree species and health
- Distance from buildings and services
- Local protection rules
- Alternative repair routes
- The condition of the entire drain
- Advice from a qualified arborist

Do not cut structural roots or remove protected vegetation without appropriate assessment.

## Can roots enter stormwater drains?

Yes. Stormwater pipes can also crack, separate or deform. Roots may combine with leaves and silt to restrict flow, causing surface flooding during rain.

Stormwater symptoms can appear only during wet weather, while wastewater blockages respond to household fixture use. Incorrect cross-connections can confuse the pattern and require professional investigation.

## Preventing future root intrusion

### Repair known defects

Sealing or replacing the root-entry point is the most direct prevention.

### Inspect after repeated blockage

Do not pay for endless clearing without obtaining evidence of the pipe condition.

### Keep unsuitable waste out of drains

Wipes, fats and sanitary products make a root obstruction develop faster by catching in the root mass.

### Plan new planting carefully

Locate known drainage routes before planting large trees. Select planting positions with mature root spread and service access in mind.

### Document repair locations

Keep CCTV footage, site markings and repair records. They are valuable during future landscaping, renovation and property sale.

## What to do when roots block the drain

1. Stop using water if wastewater is backing up.
2. Keep people and animals away from overflow.
3. Arrange [blocked-drain clearing](/blocked-drain-unblocking).
4. Request cleaning sufficient for inspection.
5. Obtain CCTV footage of the root-entry point.
6. Locate the defect from the surface.
7. Compare relining, local repair and replacement.
8. Confirm that restricted work will be completed by an authorised practitioner.

## Frequently asked questions

### How quickly do tree roots grow back in a drain?

There is no reliable universal period. Growth depends on the tree, opening, moisture and how completely roots were removed. If the defect remains open, recurrence is possible.

### Will cutting down the tree fix the blocked drain?

No. The roots and pipe damage remain underground. The drain still needs clearing, inspection and repair.

### Can hydro jetting permanently remove roots?

It can remove root material from inside the pipe. It cannot close the crack or failed joint that allowed entry.

### Can roots break a PVC drain?

Roots can enter PVC systems through damaged joints, deformation or cracks. Installation quality, bedding and ground movement influence the risk.

### Is pipe relining always suitable for roots?

No. The pipe must retain suitable shape and alignment. Collapse, major displacement and incorrect gradient may require excavation.

### Who repairs root-damaged sanitary drains in New Zealand?

Restricted sanitary drainlaying work must be handled by a person authorised for that scope. Confirm the practitioner's current licence and the work included in the quote.

title: Pipe Relining vs Drain Excavation: Which Repair Is Better?
excerpt: Compare trenchless pipe relining with drain excavation. Learn which defects each method can repair, their limitations and what to check before choosing.
content:
Pipe relining is usually better when an underground drain is cracked or leaking but retains an acceptable shape and gradient. Excavation is better when the pipe has collapsed, shifted significantly, lost its gradient or requires a connection to be rebuilt. The correct method depends on CCTV evidence and site conditions, not on which option creates less visible disruption.

Relining and excavation both have legitimate uses. Neither is automatically the cheapest or longest-lasting solution in every case.

## What is pipe relining?

Pipe relining installs a new liner inside an existing drain. The host pipe remains in the ground while the liner seals defects and creates a new internal surface.

Common relining processes involve:

1. Clearing roots and obstructions
2. Cleaning the internal pipe surface
3. Completing a CCTV inspection
4. Measuring the section
5. Preparing and installing the liner
6. Curing or setting the liner
7. Reopening required connections
8. Completing post-installation CCTV inspection

The exact system varies by product and application. [Trenchless pipe relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless) still requires access, preparation and technically competent installation.

## What is drain excavation?

Excavation exposes the defective drain from above. The damaged pipe is removed or repaired, its bedding and gradient can be corrected, and the ground or surface is reinstated.

Excavation may involve:

- Locating underground services
- Cutting concrete, asphalt or paving
- Removing soil
- Supporting the excavation where required
- Replacing pipe and fittings
- Testing and inspection
- Backfilling and compacting
- Reinstating the surface

The visible disruption is greater, but excavation provides direct access to the whole defect.

## Key differences

| Factor | Pipe relining | Excavation and replacement |
|---|---|---|
| Surface disruption | Usually lower | Usually higher |
| Existing pipe remains | Yes | Defective section removed |
| Corrects cracks and leaking joints | Often | Yes |
| Corrects pipe gradient | No | Yes |
| Suitable for full collapse | Generally no | Yes |
| Access to bedding | No | Yes |
| Junction treatment | Specialist work | Direct replacement |
| Installation speed | Often faster | Site-dependent |
| Suitability under structures | Potential advantage | May be difficult |

## When pipe relining may be suitable

### Cracks and fractures

A liner can bridge and seal many cracks where the host pipe remains sufficiently stable and aligned.

### Root intrusion through joints

Roots can be removed and the cleaned section lined to seal their entry points. Relining without complete root removal and preparation is poor practice.

### Leaking joints

A continuous liner can cover several defective joints within one run.

### Difficult surface access

Relining may avoid opening a driveway, finished floor, landscaped area or other costly surface. Access points and equipment space are still necessary.

### Widespread internal deterioration

A longer liner may treat several nearby defects where replacing each section separately would create excessive excavation.

## When excavation is usually the stronger option

### Complete pipe collapse

A liner needs a viable path and acceptable host-pipe geometry. A fully collapsed section generally requires excavation or another method to restore the pathway.

### Major joint displacement

Relining follows the existing route. It cannot realign a severely offset connection that blocks flow or prevents equipment passage.

### Incorrect gradient or a sagging drain

A liner does not lift a low section or create the correct fall. Standing wastewater caused by poor gradient requires physical correction of levels and support.

### Crushed or severely deformed pipe

Lining a restricted shape can preserve inadequate capacity. Replacement allows the diameter and alignment to be restored.

### Failed connections

Complex junctions, gully connections and fittings may need direct rebuilding. Some relining systems can reinstate branches, but the final connection must be appropriate and verifiable.

### Soil or bedding problems

If erosion, washout or poor bedding contributed to failure, the surrounding ground must be accessed and corrected.

## CCTV inspection before choosing

A meaningful comparison requires evidence. A [CCTV drain inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection) should identify:

- Defect type
- Defect length
- Pipe material and diameter
- Joint and connection positions
- Root intrusion
- Deformation
- Standing water
- Access restrictions

Heavy deposits can hide defects, so the drain may require [high-pressure cleaning](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) first.

Camera footage has limits. Levels may be needed to confirm gradient, and surface locating may be required to determine depth and repair access.

## Does relining reduce the internal diameter?

The liner occupies some internal space. A properly designed smooth liner may still provide acceptable hydraulic performance, but suitability depends on the original diameter, liner thickness, flow requirements and existing deformation.
