Relining should not be used to justify leaving an already undersized or badly restricted pipe in service.

## How long does pipe relining last?

Service-life claims depend on the lining system, installation quality, pipe environment and product evidence. A generic lifespan should not be accepted without documentation for the exact system proposed.

Ask for:

- Product identification
- Scope and liner length
- Installation method
- Warranty terms and exclusions
- Pre- and post-lining footage
- Treatment of junctions
- Evidence relevant to the intended application

A long advertised warranty is worthless if it excludes the likely failure mode or the installer disappears.

## Comparing cost correctly

Relining may cost less where excavation would require extensive surface removal and reinstatement. Excavation may cost less for a short, shallow and easily accessible defect.

Compare the complete scope:

### Relining quote

- Blockage and root removal
- Pipe cleaning
- CCTV inspection
- Surface location
- Liner installation
- Junction reinstatement
- Post-work footage
- Warranty

### Excavation quote

- Service location
- Permits or approvals where applicable
- Excavation and safety controls
- New pipe and fittings
- Testing and inspection
- Backfill and compaction
- Concrete, paving or landscaping reinstatement
- Waste removal

A cheap quote may omit the most expensive finishing work.

## Relining beneath a building or driveway

Avoiding demolition is one of relining's main advantages. A suitable drain beneath a floor, driveway or landscaped area may be repaired through existing or small access points.

Relining remains unsuitable if the underlying defect requires correction of gradient, bedding or a collapsed section. In those cases, alternative access, rerouting or targeted excavation may be necessary.

## Tree roots and relining

Roots must be removed before lining. The pipe is then cleaned and inspected to confirm that the remaining shape is suitable.

A liner can seal root-entry points, but poor preparation may trap debris, create wrinkles or fail to bond as intended. Post-work CCTV should confirm a clear internal surface and correctly treated connections.

## Drain excavation and surface reinstatement

Excavation affects more than the pipe. The quality of backfill, compaction and surface reinstatement matters. Poor compaction can cause later settlement in driveways, paths and lawns.

The quote should state who is responsible for:

- Concrete cutting and replacement
- Paving
- Asphalt
- Landscaping
- Fence or deck removal
- Traffic management
- Disposal of contaminated soil or waste

Do not assume “reinstatement included” means the surface will match its previous finish exactly.

## Can only part of the drain be repaired?

Yes. Localised excavation or sectional lining may treat a specific defect. The rest of the drain should still be inspected so the repair does not ignore nearby failures.

A hybrid solution may be best: excavate a collapsed junction and reline an adjacent cracked run.

## Consent, authorisation and documentation

Drain repairs must comply with applicable New Zealand requirements. Whether consent, council involvement or inspection is needed depends on the work scope and local circumstances.

Restricted sanitary drainlaying must be carried out or supervised by a person authorised for that work. Confirm the current licence rather than accepting vague claims such as “qualified trades team.”

Keep:

- The written scope
- Drain plans if available
- Pre-work CCTV footage
- Product information
- Approval and inspection records where relevant
- Post-work footage
- Warranty documents

## Decision checklist

Choose relining only after confirming:

- The pipe is not fully collapsed
- Gradient is acceptable
- Deformation does not create unacceptable restriction
- Roots and deposits can be removed
- Connections can be treated correctly
- Product and installer evidence is credible
- Post-work inspection is included

Choose excavation when:

- Levels need correction
- The pipe or junction has collapsed
- Bedding or surrounding ground has failed
- Direct access is needed to rebuild a connection
- The defect is shallow and accessible
- Relining would preserve a serious alignment problem

## Frequently asked questions

### Is pipe relining always cheaper than excavation?

No. It can save reinstatement costs under expensive surfaces, but a short accessible excavation may cost less. Compare complete scopes.

### Can a collapsed pipe be relined?

A fully collapsed pipe normally lacks the pathway and shape needed for conventional relining. The collapsed area may need excavation before any adjacent sections are lined.

### Does relining stop tree roots?

A correctly installed suitable liner can seal cracks and joints where roots entered. All roots and debris must first be removed.

### Can relining correct a drain that runs uphill?

No. The liner follows the existing pipe. Incorrect gradient requires physical realignment or replacement.

### Is CCTV required after relining?

Post-installation footage is important for confirming that the liner is open, connections are reinstated and no obvious installation defect remains.

### Who can repair sanitary drains in New Zealand?

Restricted drainlaying must be completed within the appropriate New Zealand authorisation framework. Check the practitioner's current licence for the relevant trade.

title: Stormwater Drains vs Wastewater Drains in New Zealand
excerpt: Learn the difference between stormwater and wastewater drains in New Zealand, common blockage signs, ownership issues and why cross-connections matter.
content:
Stormwater drains carry rain from roofs, channels and outdoor surfaces. Wastewater drains carry contaminated water from toilets, showers, basins, kitchens and laundries. The two systems must remain separate because stormwater can overload the wastewater network, while wastewater entering stormwater pipes can discharge pollution into waterways.

Different blockages, responsibilities and repair rules apply to each system. Identifying which network is affected is the first step.

## What goes into a stormwater drain?

Stormwater systems collect rainwater from:

- Roof gutters and downpipes
- Yard and channel drains
- Driveways and paved areas where designed
- Road channels and public catchpits
- Subsoil drainage where approved

The water generally travels to a soakage system, watercourse or public stormwater network. It does not receive the same treatment as household sewage, so chemicals, paint, oil and wastewater must not enter it.

## What goes into a wastewater drain?

Wastewater systems receive discharge from:

- Toilets
- Showers and baths
- Bathroom basins
- Kitchen sinks and dishwashers
- Laundry tubs and washing machines
- Other approved sanitary fixtures and appliances

This water contains human waste, food residue, detergents and other contamination. It travels to a wastewater treatment system rather than directly to the environment.

## Why the systems must stay separate

Stormwater entering wastewater pipes increases flow during rain. The added volume can overload pipes and treatment facilities, contributing to manhole overflow and contaminated discharge.

Wastewater entering stormwater pipes can travel toward streams, rivers, harbours or the coast without proper treatment. Even a small cross-connection can create persistent pollution.

A roof downpipe should not discharge into a wastewater gully trap. Waste pipes should not connect to roof-water drainage. Suspected cross-connections require investigation by an appropriately authorised practitioner.

## How to recognise a stormwater blockage

Stormwater problems usually appear during or after rain.

Common signs include:

- Gutters overflowing despite being clear
- Water backing up from outdoor grates
- Ponding around downpipes
- Surface flooding near a catchpit
- Water appearing around a soakage area
- Downpipes making prolonged gurgling sounds
- Erosion or subsidence over a buried line

Leaves, roof debris, silt, roots, crushed pipes and blocked outlets are common causes. [Stormwater drain installation and repair](/stormwater-drain-installation) may be required when cleaning does not restore normal discharge.

## How to recognise a wastewater blockage

Wastewater symptoms respond to household water use rather than rainfall.

- Toilets, showers and sinks drain slowly together
- A toilet gurgles when another fixture empties
- Water rises through a floor waste
- An external gully trap overflows
- Sewage odour appears around several fixtures
- Wastewater returns when the washing machine pumps out

Stop using fixtures if wastewater is backing up. Arrange [blocked-drain unblocking](/blocked-drain-unblocking) and keep people away from contaminated overflow.

## What if the problem occurs only during rain?

Wet-weather wastewater overflow may indicate stormwater infiltration, damaged wastewater pipes or a public-network capacity issue. Rain can enter through:

- Incorrect downpipe connections
- Low or defective gully traps
- Cracked private laterals
- Unsealed inspection points
- Cross-connections

The timing is evidence, not proof. CCTV inspection, testing and drainage plans may be needed.

## Gully traps are part of wastewater drainage

A gully trap receives wastewater from selected fixtures and prevents foul air escaping through its water seal. It is not a stormwater inlet.

Surface water, soil and leaves should be kept out. New Zealand Building Code guidance requires gully traps to be positioned and constructed to limit surface-water and foreign-material entry.

An overflowing gully commonly means a blockage downstream. Sealing the top can force wastewater to overflow inside the building.

## Common stormwater blockage causes

### Leaves and roof debris

Leaves collect in gutters, downpipes and grates. A leaf guard can reduce entry but also needs maintenance.

### Silt and soil

Runoff carries sediment into outdoor drains. Construction and landscaping can sharply increase the load.

### Tree roots

Roots enter cracks and failed joints. Clearing roots restores flow temporarily; the defect must be repaired to prevent recurrence.

### Crushed or deformed pipes

Vehicle loading, poor bedding and ground movement can deform buried pipework.

### Blocked soakage

A soak pit or disposal field may lose performance because of sediment, groundwater or unsuitable site conditions. Cleaning the inlet pipe will not fix a failed disposal area.

## Common wastewater blockage causes

- Wet wipes and sanitary products
- Excess toilet paper
- Cooking fat and grease
- Food waste
- Tree roots
- Foreign objects
- Cracked or collapsed drains
- Poor gradient or displaced joints

Recurring blockage requires [CCTV drain inspection](/cctv-drain-inspection), not endless basic clearing.

## Who is responsible for private drains?

Property owners are generally responsible for drainage assets on private property, but wastewater-lateral boundaries differ between councils. Some authorities maintain specific sections outside a property boundary; others place more responsibility on the owner.

Stormwater laterals serving an individual property are commonly private up to their discharge connection, but local policy must be checked.

Use this sequence:

1. Identify the affected system.
2. Locate the blockage or defect.
3. Establish whether it is private or public.
4. Check the relevant council or water authority policy.
5. Retain CCTV footage and location records.

Do not assume the council pays merely because a pipe runs outside the fence.

## What is a drainage cross-connection?

A cross-connection sends discharge into the wrong network. Examples include:

- A roof downpipe connected to a wastewater gully
- A washing machine discharging into stormwater
- A wastewater pipe joined to a stormwater lateral
- A stormwater sump connected to the public sewer without approval

Smoke testing, dye testing, CCTV inspection and drainage plans can help identify incorrect connections. Testing must be controlled so contaminants are not introduced into water networks.

## Can stormwater and wastewater use the same repair company?

The same business may offer both services, but licensing scope matters. Sanitary drainlaying is regulated in New Zealand. Stormwater work may also interact with building consent, council assets and authorised drainlaying requirements depending on its scope.

Confirm who will perform the work, their authorisation and who handles council coordination.

## Drain cleaning methods

### Mechanical cleaning

Cutters and cables remove roots or penetrate selected obstructions.

### Hydro jetting

[High-pressure drain cleaning](/hydro-jetter-drain-cleaning) removes grease, silt and loose deposits. The discharge route must be safe.

### CCTV inspection

Camera footage records cracks, roots, displacement and debris after sufficient cleaning.

### Excavation or relining

Damaged pipes may need local excavation, replacement or [trenchless relining](/pipe-relining-trenchless). Incorrect gradient and full collapse generally require physical correction.

## Preventing stormwater problems

- Clear gutters and downpipes
- Remove debris from outdoor grates
- Control soil during landscaping
- Keep paint, oil and chemicals out of drains
- Protect pipes from vehicle loading
- Investigate repeated ponding
- Maintain soakage systems appropriately
- Confirm new roof drainage has an approved discharge route

## Preventing wastewater problems

- Flush only human waste and toilet paper
- Keep wipes and sanitary products out of toilets
- Keep fat and food scraps out of sinks
- Repair root-entry points
- Investigate recurring gurgling
- Keep stormwater out of gully traps
- Use authorised practitioners for restricted work

## Information to record before arranging drainage work

Note whether the issue occurs during rain or during fixture use, which grates or gullies overflow, how long the problem has existed and whether neighbouring properties are affected. Photograph visible overflow from a safe distance. Locate available drainage plans and previous repair records. This evidence helps the contractor bring suitable cleaning, camera and locating equipment instead of treating every drainage fault as the same blockage.

## Frequently asked questions

### Can rainwater go into the sewer?

Not through an unauthorised connection. Stormwater and wastewater are separate systems. Roof water entering the sewer increases wet-weather overloading.

### Is a gully trap a stormwater drain?

No. A sanitary gully trap receives wastewater and blocks foul air. Roof downpipes and surface runoff should not discharge into it.

### Why does my wastewater gully overflow when it rains?

The system may have stormwater infiltration, a cross-connection, damaged pipework or a downstream network problem. Inspection is required to identify the source.

### Who fixes a blocked stormwater lateral?

Responsibility depends on asset location and local authority policy. Private property owners commonly maintain laterals serving their site, but the boundary must be confirmed locally.

### Can wastewater enter a stream through stormwater pipes?

Yes, if an incorrect connection or pipe defect allows it. Stormwater often discharges to the environment without wastewater treatment.

### Should a blocked public manhole be reported?

Yes. Keep away from the overflow and report it to the relevant council or water authority. Do not open or enter a public manhole.

title: Hot Water Cylinder Repair vs Replacement: Which Is Better?
excerpt: Compare hot water cylinder repair and replacement. Learn which faults are repairable, when replacement makes more sense and what NZ homeowners should check.
content:
A hot water cylinder should usually be repaired when the tank remains sound and the fault involves an accessible valve, thermostat, element or external connection. Replacement is normally stronger when the tank itself leaks, corrosion is extensive, faults recur or the existing system no longer meets the household's capacity and pressure needs.

Diagnosis comes before price comparison. Replacing a minor component unnecessarily wastes money. Repeatedly repairing a failing tank does the same.

## Faults that may be repairable

### Faulty electric element

An electric element can fail while the cylinder remains structurally sound. Symptoms include no hot water, slow reheating or reduced usable capacity when a cylinder has more than one element.

Electrical testing must be completed safely. A plumber may address water-side work while a registered electrical worker may be required for electrical components.

### Thermostat or control fault

A defective thermostat can cause insufficient heating, overheating or erratic operation. Do not increase settings to compensate for an undiagnosed fault.

New Zealand guidance requires stored hot water to be maintained at a temperature that controls Legionella risk, while delivery temperature must limit scalding. Temperature control devices should be assessed rather than bypassed.

### Valve problems

Inlet control valves, pressure-reducing valves, tempering valves and relief valves can wear, stick or leak. The correct replacement must suit the system pressure and design.

Water discharging from a relief line is not automatically a leaking tank. Relief valves are safety components and may release water under defined conditions. Continuous or abnormal discharge requires assessment.

### Leaking external connection

A leak from accessible pipework or a fitting near the cylinder may be repairable without replacing the vessel. Water can travel along pipework, so the visible drip point is not always the source.

### Damaged insulation or pipe lagging

External insulation can sometimes be improved, provided safety clearances, valves and access remain correct. This will not solve an internal tank defect.

## Faults that usually support replacement

### The tank itself leaks

Corrosion or a split in the storage vessel is not a routine valve repair. Patching a pressurised hot-water tank is not a credible long-term solution. Isolate the system safely and arrange [hot water cylinder replacement](/hot-water-cylinder-installation).

### Rust and corrosion are widespread

Rust around seams, fittings or the cylinder base can indicate ongoing deterioration. Surface staining needs diagnosis, but active tank corrosion makes future leakage likely.

### Repairs keep recurring

Separate failures over a short period may indicate that the system is nearing the end of practical service. Add the recent repair costs and disruption before authorising another isolated fix.

### Capacity is inadequate

A sound small cylinder may still be unsuitable after household size, bathrooms or usage patterns change. Running out of hot water is not always a fault; storage and recovery may simply be insufficient.

### A pressure upgrade is required

Older low-pressure systems can limit shower performance and tap selection. Moving to mains pressure may require a new cylinder, valve set and assessment of existing pipework and fixtures.

### Efficiency upgrade is planned

Replacement creates an opportunity to compare an efficient electric cylinder, heat-pump water heater or other suitable system. The best option depends on household demand, climate, space, electrical supply and fuel availability.

## How cylinder age affects the decision

Age matters, but it is not a diagnosis. A well-performing older cylinder should not be replaced solely because it passed an arbitrary birthday. A younger cylinder with tank leakage may still require replacement.

Consider:

- Tank condition
- Service history
- Availability of correct parts
- Recent repair spending
- Energy performance
- Household demand
- Pressure requirements
- Installation compliance

## Repair vs replacement comparison

| Factor | Repair favoured | Replacement favoured |
|---|---|---|
| Tank condition | Sound and dry | Leaking or badly corroded |
| Fault | Isolated component | Vessel or repeated faults |
| Capacity | Adequate | Regularly inadequate |
| Pressure | Acceptable | Upgrade desired |
| Parts | Available | Obsolete or unsuitable |
| Recent spending | Minimal | Multiple repairs |
| Downtime | Short component job | Planned system change |

## Low-pressure and mains-pressure systems

Low-pressure cylinders use controls that limit incoming pressure. They can perform reliably but may deliver weaker flow than a mains-pressure system.

A mains-pressure cylinder can support stronger flow and a wider choice of tapware, subject to water supply and pipe capacity. Conversion is not just a cylinder swap. Existing pipes, mixers, valves, relief drainage and available space must be checked.

See [water-pressure troubleshooting and upgrades](/water-pressure-upgrades) before assuming the cylinder causes every weak shower.

## Heat-pump water heater as a replacement option

Heat-pump systems transfer heat from surrounding air into stored water. They can use substantially less purchased energy than conventional electric resistance heating, but installation cost is higher.

Check:

- Outdoor or plant-space location
- Operating noise
- Cold-climate performance
- Cylinder capacity
- Condensate drainage
- Electrical requirements
- Backup heating
- Warranty and servicing support

[Heat-pump water heater installation](/heat-pump-water-heater) makes sense when the expected savings fit the household's usage and ownership period.

## Safety and temperature control

New Zealand government guidance states that storage-cylinder water should be at least 60°C to control Legionella growth. Water supplied to personal-hygiene fixtures must be limited to reduce scalding risk. Tempering valves or other approved devices manage delivery temperature.

Do not lower the cylinder thermostat as a casual energy-saving method. A safe system separates storage-temperature control from delivery-temperature control.

## What a replacement quote should include

- Cylinder brand, model, capacity and pressure class
- Removal and disposal of the old cylinder
- Valve set and required controls
- Seismic restraint where applicable
- Safe tray and drainage where required
- Relief-pipe arrangement
- Tempering or delivery-temperature control
- Pipe alterations and insulation
- Electrical work
- Access modifications
- Testing and commissioning
- Warranty documentation

If a quote says only “replace cylinder,” it is too vague for a proper comparison.

## Hidden costs to check

- Difficult cupboard access
- Floor or wall repair
- Upgrading old pipework
- Switchboard or circuit changes
- Moving the cylinder
- New outdoor base for heat-pump equipment
- Roofing work for removed vent pipes
- Consent or inspection requirements where applicable

## When the problem is urgent

Use [hot water cylinder repair](/hot-water-cylinder-repair) promptly when:

- Water is spreading from the cylinder area
- The tank or connections are visibly leaking
- Relief discharge is continuous or abnormal
- Water is excessively hot
- Electrical components are wet
- The cylinder has moved or its restraint has failed
- There are signs of overheating or unusual noise

Do not touch wet electrical equipment. Isolate power only if it can be done safely from a dry location.

## Questions to ask the contractor

- Is the tank leaking or is the leak external?
- Which component failed?
- What evidence supports replacement?
- Will the new system be low or mains pressure?
- Is current household demand included in sizing?
- Who completes electrical work?
- How will storage and delivery temperatures be controlled?
- What warranty applies to the cylinder and labour?
- Are removal and reinstatement included?

## Plan for downtime

A cylinder repair may still require water or power isolation. Replacement can leave the property without hot water while plumbing, electrical work and commissioning are completed. Ask for the expected shutdown, how the old unit will be drained and whether the new system can be fully commissioned on the same visit. Do not assume a delivered cylinder means the installation is operational.

## Frequently asked questions

### Can a leaking hot water cylinder be repaired?

An external fitting or valve may be repairable. Leakage from the storage vessel normally requires replacement.

### Is an old cylinder automatically inefficient?

Not automatically. Insulation, standing losses, controls and usage affect performance. Compare actual condition and replacement economics.

### Will a new cylinder improve shower pressure?

Only if the replacement changes the pressure system and the rest of the plumbing supports the flow. A like-for-like low-pressure replacement may not change shower performance.

### Can the cylinder temperature be lowered to save power?

Stored water must remain hot enough to control Legionella risk. Use correct controls and professional advice rather than lowering it casually.

### Is a heat-pump water heater always best?

No. It offers strong efficiency but has higher purchase cost, space, noise and climate considerations. Suitability depends on the property and usage.

### Who can replace a hot water cylinder in New Zealand?

Restricted plumbing must be handled within the PGDB licensing framework, and electrical or gas work requires the relevant authorised trades.

title: Why Is My Hot Water Cylinder Leaking?
excerpt: Identify common hot water cylinder leak sources, including valves, fittings, relief pipes and tank corrosion, and learn what to do immediately.
content:
A hot water cylinder may leak from an external fitting, control valve, relief line or the storage tank itself. Valve and pipe leaks can often be repaired. Water escaping through corrosion, a split seam or the cylinder body normally means the vessel requires replacement.

Water can travel along pipework and insulation before dripping, so the lowest wet point is not always the source.

## Immediate actions

### Keep clear of electricity

Do not touch wet switches, wiring or cylinder controls. Isolate power at the switchboard only when the board and access route are dry and safe. Otherwise contact an electrician or emergency service provider.

### Reduce water damage

Move belongings away and contain water with towels or a suitable tray if this can be done safely. Do not block a relief pipe or valve outlet.

### Stop using hot water

Continued use can add pressure, heat and water to the problem. Arrange [hot water cylinder repair](/hot-water-cylinder-repair) promptly.

### Locate the main water control

Know where the property's water shut-off sits, but do not operate unfamiliar cylinder valves randomly. Incorrect isolation can create other problems.

## Common leak locations

### Inlet and outlet connections

Threaded fittings, unions and seals can loosen or deteriorate. Condensation can also mimic a small leak. A plumber should identify the exact source before tightening or replacing components.

### Temperature and pressure relief valve

A TPR valve protects the system by releasing water if temperature or pressure exceeds safe limits. Some discharge can occur as designed, but continuous flow or a sudden change requires assessment.

Never cap, plug or isolate a relief outlet. Doing so removes a critical safety path.

### Cold-water expansion or pressure-control valve

Control valves can discharge or leak because of wear, debris, pressure problems or incorrect selection. The replacement must match the system design.

### Tempering valve

A tempering valve mixes hot and cold water to limit delivery temperature. Leakage can develop at the body, unions or nearby pipework. Temperature instability may occur at the same time.

### Drain or relief pipe

Water seen outside may come from a relief drain rather than a broken supply pipe. Observe when it flows and whether the discharge is continuous. Do not alter the pipe arrangement.

### Cylinder tank

Tank leakage may appear at a seam, element opening, connection or base. Water pooling beneath an older cylinder with corrosion often indicates vessel failure.

## Is it a leak or condensation?

Condensation forms when humid air meets a cold surface. It can occur on cold pipes near the cylinder but should not produce a persistent hot-water flow or growing pool.

Dry accessible surfaces and observe carefully without removing covers. A recurring wet area, rust track or active drip needs professional diagnosis.

## Why relief pipes drip

Possible causes include:

- Normal thermal expansion
- A worn valve
- Debris preventing full closure
- Excess incoming pressure
- Incorrect valve setting
- Thermostat or overheating problem
- System configuration fault

The discharge pattern matters. A small intermittent release during heating differs from continuous heavy flow. Both should be evaluated against the system design.

## Signs the tank has failed

- Rust or staining from the cylinder body
- Water emerging beneath insulation
- Leakage from a seam
- Persistent pooling at the base
- Repeated corrosion around connections
- A leak that continues after external fittings are ruled out

A failed storage vessel is generally replaced rather than patched. Request a [cylinder installation and replacement](/hot-water-cylinder-installation) assessment.

## Why corrosion occurs

Corrosion depends on cylinder material, water chemistry, temperature, protective components, installation and age. External moisture can also corrode the casing or fittings.

Do not assume all rust means the tank has failed. Surface corrosion on an external component differs from perforation of the storage vessel. The source must be exposed and identified safely.

## Can high water pressure cause leakage?

Excessive or unstable pressure can stress valves, fittings and appliances. A defective pressure-reducing or pressure-limiting valve can contribute.

Pressure should be measured rather than guessed from a strong shower. [Water-pressure troubleshooting](/water-pressure-upgrades) checks supply pressure, flow and control-valve performance.

## Can a leaking valve be replaced without replacing the cylinder?

Often, yes, when the tank remains sound and the correct component is available. Replacement should include testing of related controls rather than treating the visible valve in isolation.

A failed valve may be a symptom of debris, pressure or overheating. Installing another identical part without checking the cause can lead to repeat failure.

## Safe-tray and drainage issues

A safe tray beneath a cylinder can limit damage when correctly installed with an appropriate drain route. A tray is not a repair and should not hold standing water under normal operation.

Overflow from a tray indicates an active fault or inadequate drainage. Concealed leaks can damage flooring and framing before water becomes visible.

## Repair or replace?

Repair is usually reasonable when:

- Leakage comes from an accessible external fitting
- A replaceable valve has failed
- The tank is structurally sound
- The system meets household needs
- Other components remain serviceable

Replacement is usually reasonable when:

- The vessel leaks
- Corrosion is extensive
- Several faults have occurred
- Capacity is inadequate
- Pressure conversion is planned
- Parts are unavailable or unsuitable

## Information to give the plumber

- Where water first became visible
- Whether it is hot or cold
- Whether leakage is continuous
- Whether it changes while heating
- Cylinder brand, model and approximate age
- Recent valve or electrical work
- Any unusual noise or temperature change
- Photos taken from a safe distance

Do not remove electrical covers to obtain information.

## Preventing water damage

- Inspect the cylinder cupboard periodically
- Keep stored items away from valves and pipework
- Investigate rust and dampness early
- Maintain clear access to isolation controls
- Do not obstruct tray or relief drains
- Use authorised tradespeople for repairs
- Keep service and warranty documents

## What happens during professional diagnosis

The attending practitioner should first make the area safe and confirm whether the water is coming from the vessel, a valve, an external joint or another nearby pipe. The process may include isolating the heater, drying accessible surfaces, observing the leak during controlled refill or heating, checking supply pressure and testing temperature controls.

A useful diagnosis states the failed component and cause. “The cylinder is old” is not enough evidence for replacement when the visible leak comes from an external fitting. The opposite is also true: replacing a valve will not solve water escaping through a corroded vessel.

### After a tank leak

Water can remain beneath flooring or inside the cylinder cupboard after the source is isolated. Check adjacent rooms and the ceiling below. Particleboard, insulation and wall linings may hold moisture. Drying and building repairs can be separate from plumbing replacement.

### After a valve leak

The replacement valve should match pressure, temperature and connection requirements. Related controls should be checked, particularly when excess pressure or overheating may have contributed. Ask the plumber to explain normal future relief discharge so it is not confused with another fault.

### After replacement

Confirm that the new cylinder is commissioned, delivery temperature is controlled, relief and tray drains are unobstructed, valves are accessible and electrical work is complete. Retain model, warranty and trade documentation.

## Frequently asked questions

Before closing the job, check the cylinder area again after a full heating cycle. Some faults appear only as water expands. Confirm that no stored items obstruct valves, the relief outlet remains visible and the cupboard is dry.

### Is a dripping relief pipe normal?

Some systems discharge small amounts during heating, but continuous or changed discharge requires assessment. Never cap the pipe.

### Can I keep using a leaking cylinder?

No general assurance is safe. Water can damage the property or reach electrical components. Stop use and arrange prompt diagnosis.

### Why is water leaking only while the cylinder heats?

Thermal expansion or a pressure-control problem may cause discharge during heating. The control and relief system should be tested.

### Does rust mean replacement is required?

Rust on the tank body with active leakage supports replacement. Surface rust on an external fitting may be repairable. Confirm the source.

### Can sealant repair a cylinder leak?

Sealant is not a proper repair for a leaking pressurised storage vessel. Failed tanks require appropriate replacement.

### Who handles electrical parts of the cylinder?

Electrical testing and fixed wiring require an appropriately authorised electrical worker. Plumbing work requires the relevant licensed plumbing practitioner.

title: What to Do When Your Home Has No Hot Water
excerpt: Learn why a New Zealand home may lose hot water, which safe checks to make and when a plumber, electrician or gasfitter is required.
content:
No hot water can result from a power interruption, failed element or thermostat, gas-supply problem, control fault, empty cylinder, leaking system or damaged water heater. The correct trade depends on whether the system is electric, gas, heat pump, solar or another type.

Do not open electrical covers, bypass safety controls or relight unfamiliar gas equipment. Start with safe external observations.

## Step 1: Check whether all outlets are affected

Test one other hot tap briefly. If only one fixture is affected, the problem may involve its mixer, cartridge, tempering arrangement or local pipework rather than the water heater.

If every hot tap is cold, investigate the central system.

## Step 2: Identify the system type

Look for labels without removing covers.

- Electric storage cylinder
- Gas continuous-flow heater
- Gas storage cylinder
- Heat-pump water heater
- Solar system with backup
- Wetback or another combined heat source

The system type determines who should attend. Gasfitting requires an authorised gasfitter; fixed electrical work requires an authorised electrical worker; restricted plumbing requires the appropriate plumber.

## Electric-cylinder causes

### Power interruption

A local outage, controlled-load schedule or switched-off circuit can stop heating. Check whether other circuits are working and consult the electricity provider's outage information.

Do not repeatedly reset a breaker. A breaker that trips again indicates a fault requiring an electrician.

### Failed heating element

An element can fail completely or partially. The cylinder may remain cold, reheat slowly or run out unusually quickly.

### Thermostat fault

A thermostat may fail to call for heat or may trip a safety cut-out. Covers should be opened only by an authorised person.

### Wiring or connection fault

Loose, damaged or overheated electrical components require urgent isolation and electrical assessment.

## Gas-water-heater causes

### Gas supply interruption

An empty LPG cylinder, closed supply or network issue can stop heating. Follow supplier guidance and do not alter regulators or fittings.

### Ignition or flame fault

Modern systems may display an error code. Record it and check the manufacturer manual. Repeated ignition failure requires a gasfitter.

### Water-flow problem

Continuous-flow heaters need adequate flow to activate. A restricted filter, low supply or fixture issue can prevent firing.

### Flue or safety shutdown

Do not bypass an error or safety lockout. Gas appliances require safe combustion and ventilation. Arrange [gas water heater repair](/gas-water-heater-repair).

If gas is smelled, avoid switches and flames, leave the area, follow the gas supplier's emergency instructions and obtain authorised assistance.

## Heat-pump water-heater causes

A heat-pump system may stop because of:

- Power loss
- Controller settings
- Compressor or fan fault
- Sensor error
- Refrigeration fault
- Backup-element failure
- Very high demand

Record error codes and operating sounds. Do not remove covers or interfere with refrigerant equipment. Contact the appropriate service provider for [heat-pump water heater repair](/heat-pump-water-heater).

## Solar-system causes

Cloudy weather alone should not leave a correctly configured system without hot water if its backup source is available. Possible faults include a circulation pump, controller, sensor, valve, backup element or gas booster.

Solar systems can contain very hot water and roof-mounted components. Do not climb onto the roof for diagnosis.

## The cylinder may simply be depleted

Storage systems hold a finite volume. Several showers, baths or appliance cycles can use the available hot water faster than it reheats.

Ask:

- Was usage unusually high?
- Has household size changed?
- Does the cylinder recover after its normal heating period?
- Has usable duration gradually decreased?

Repeated depletion under normal demand may indicate incorrect sizing, slow recovery, mixing-valve problems or an element fault.

## Check for leaks

Look from a safe distance for:

- Water around the cylinder
- Continuous relief-pipe discharge
- Wet ceilings or walls near hot pipes
- An outdoor hot-water leak
- Unusual meter movement

A significant leak can empty heated water and create damage. Use [water leak detection](/water-leak-detection) when the source is concealed.

## Water is warm but not hot

Possible causes include:

- One failed element in a multi-element system
- Thermostat or sensor fault
- Tempering-valve problem
- Cold-water crossover through a mixer
- Excessive demand
- Heat-pump backup problem
- Incorrect control settings

Temperature should be measured and controls tested. Do not raise storage temperature casually.

## Hot water at some taps but not others

A local mixer or cartridge can fail. Debris may restrict the hot side. In some systems, a defective mixer allows cold water to push into hot pipework.

This is a fixture-distribution problem rather than proof of cylinder failure.

## Safe checks homeowners can make

- Confirm whether all hot outlets are affected
- Check for a known power or gas outage
- Read visible controller error codes
- Check whether LPG supply is empty without altering fittings
- Look for external leaks
- Review recent high usage
- Find the model and service documents

## Checks homeowners should not make

- Opening electrical covers
- Resetting protection repeatedly
- Bypassing thermostats or cut-outs
- Blocking relief pipes
- Altering gas regulators
- Dismantling burners or flues
- Changing safety-valve settings
- Lowering storage temperature below safe requirements

## Storage and delivery temperature

New Zealand guidance requires storage-cylinder water at not less than 60°C to reduce Legionella risk. Delivery temperature at personal-hygiene outlets must be limited to reduce scalding. A tempering valve or other device typically separates these two requirements.

Cold taps do not prove that the cylinder thermostat should be increased. The system needs diagnosis.

## Which trade should you call?

### Plumber

For cylinder leaks, valves, water connections, tempering devices and restricted plumbing.

### Electrician

For elements, thermostats, fixed wiring, breakers and electrical controls.

### Gasfitter

For gas supply after the point of connection, burners, gas controls, flues and gas appliance work.

Some businesses coordinate more than one licensed trade. Confirm the person attending has the correct authorisation.

## When no hot water is urgent

Use prompt or [emergency plumbing assistance](/emergency-plumbing) when:

- The cylinder is leaking heavily
- Electrical components are wet
- Water is excessively hot
- A relief pipe discharges heavily
- Gas is smelled
- A gas alarm activates
- Vulnerable occupants lack essential hot water
- The fault follows flooding or earthquake movement

For suspected gas leakage, prioritise evacuation and the gas supplier's emergency process rather than a normal booking form.

## Information that speeds up diagnosis

Before booking, record the heater brand, model, energy source and visible error code. Note when hot water last worked, whether it failed suddenly or gradually, whether any power or plumbing work occurred and whether water is completely cold or only lukewarm. Check if every outlet is affected.

For an electric cylinder, mention repeated breaker trips without resetting it again. For LPG, state whether other gas appliances operate and whether bottle supply was recently changed. For a heat pump, describe fan or compressor behaviour. This information helps send the correct trade and reduces wasted visits.

## After hot water is restored

Test several outlets cautiously and confirm that temperature is stable. Check the cylinder area and relief outlet for leakage. Ask what failed, whether the repair is temporary, what maintenance is required and whether related components were tested. Keep electrical, gas and plumbing documentation with the heater records.

Do not accept a repair that restores heat by bypassing a thermostat, tempering valve, relief device or gas safety control.

## Frequently asked questions

If the fault returns, record the time, error code and recent water use instead of repeatedly resetting equipment. Intermittent evidence helps diagnose sensors, supply interruptions and control faults.

### Why did my hot water suddenly stop?

Sudden loss commonly involves power, an element, thermostat, gas supply, ignition or a major leak. Check the system type and external signs.

### How long should a cylinder take to reheat?

It depends on cylinder volume, element power, starting temperature and control schedule. Compare performance with its normal recovery rather than a generic time.

### Can I reset the hot water cylinder?

Do not open covers or repeatedly reset protection. A trip may indicate an electrical or overheating fault.

### Why is only the shower cold?

The shower mixer, cartridge or local supply may be faulty when other hot taps work normally.

### Can I turn the thermostat higher?

Not as a troubleshooting shortcut. Storage and delivery temperatures have health and scalding requirements. Have the system tested.

### Who repairs a gas water heater?

Gas appliance and gasfitting work requires an appropriately authorised gasfitter, with certification requirements applying to completed gasfitting work.

title: Low-Pressure vs Mains-Pressure Hot Water Systems
excerpt: Compare low-pressure and mains-pressure hot water systems in New Zealand, including flow, tapware, conversion requirements and common faults.
content:
Low-pressure hot water systems deliver water at a controlled pressure below the incoming mains supply. Mains-pressure systems supply hot and cold water at more closely matched pressures, usually producing stronger flow and supporting a wider range of mixers. Upgrading requires more than replacing the cylinder because existing pipes, valves, fixtures and relief arrangements must tolerate the new pressure.

## How to identify a low-pressure system

Common indicators include:

- A low-pressure label on the cylinder
- A vent pipe extending through the roof
- A header tank in the roof space
- Strong cold flow but noticeably weaker hot flow
- Low-pressure tapware or shower mixers
- A pressure-reducing valve on the cylinder supply

These signs are not conclusive individually. A plumber can measure pressure and identify the system configuration.

## How a mains-pressure system differs

A mains-pressure storage cylinder is designed for a higher working pressure and uses appropriate inlet controls, relief valves and pipework. Hot flow can be stronger and better balanced with cold water.

Benefits may include:

- Improved shower flow
- Faster bath filling
- Better simultaneous fixture use
- Wider tapware selection
- More consistent mixer operation

Performance still depends on incoming pressure, supply-pipe size, fixture flow limits and household demand. “Mains pressure” does not guarantee unlimited flow.

## Low-pressure system advantages

- Existing system may remain economical to maintain
- Lower stress on older internal pipework
- Adequate performance for modest demand
- No upgrade cost when users are satisfied
- Some systems work effectively with suitable low-pressure fixtures

A functioning low-pressure cylinder should not be replaced merely because mains pressure is newer.

## Low-pressure system disadvantages

- Weak shower flow with unsuitable fixtures
- Limited mixer and tapware options
- Poor performance when several outlets run
- Imbalance between hot and cold pressure
- Longer filling time for baths and sinks

Some problems come from blocked strainers, old valves or restrictive pipework rather than the cylinder pressure class.

## Mains-pressure advantages

- Stronger potential flow
- Better hot-and-cold balance
- Greater fixture compatibility
- Improved performance across multiple bathrooms when correctly sized
- Easier integration with common modern mixers

## Mains-pressure disadvantages

- Higher conversion cost
- Leaks may appear in old pipes and fittings
- More water can be used through higher-flow fixtures
- Correct valves and relief drainage are essential
- The cylinder needs suitable structural support and restraint

## Why the hot-water pressure is low

Before planning conversion, check for:

- A blocked shower head or aerator
- Failing pressure-control valve
- Partially closed isolation valve
- Corroded galvanised pipe
- Undersized supply pipe
- Faulty mixer cartridge
- Debris in a strainer
- A kinked flexible connection
- Low incoming water pressure

[Water-pressure troubleshooting](/water-pressure-upgrades) should distinguish pressure from flow. High static pressure with poor flow can indicate restriction.

## What a conversion involves

A proper assessment may cover:

1. Incoming water pressure and flow
2. Existing pipe material and condition
3. Cylinder location and access
4. Cylinder capacity
5. Valve and relief requirements
6. Safe-tray drainage
7. Tempering control
8. Fixture and mixer compatibility
9. Electrical capacity
10. Seismic restraint

The project may require [hot water cylinder installation](/hot-water-cylinder-installation), new valves and selected pipe replacement.

## Can old pipes handle mains pressure?

Not automatically. Older joints, flexible hoses, galvanised pipes and hidden fittings may fail when pressure increases. Inspection cannot guarantee that every concealed connection is sound, but obvious high-risk components should be identified before conversion.

The quote should explain whether pipework upgrades and post-conversion leak checks are included.

## Will mains pressure fix every shower?

No. Shower performance also depends on:

- Supply-pipe diameter
- Mixer design
- Shower head flow rate
- Tempering valve capacity
- Simultaneous water use
- Incoming mains flow

An inefficient or restricted shower mixer can remain weak after cylinder replacement.

## Cylinder sizing

Higher flow can empty a cylinder faster. Sizing should consider household occupants, number of bathrooms, bath use, simultaneous demand and recovery rate.

A larger cylinder is not automatically better. It needs space, support and energy to maintain stored water.

## Temperature safety

Stored water in a cylinder must remain at a temperature that controls Legionella risk. Delivery to personal-hygiene fixtures must be limited to reduce scalding. A correctly selected tempering valve or other approved device manages delivery temperature.

Pressure conversion must not bypass temperature safety controls.

## Alternatives to conversion

- Repair a faulty pressure-control valve
- Replace restrictive shower components
- Install tapware designed for low pressure
- Upgrade selected pipe runs
- Use a suitable booster solution where lawful and technically appropriate
- Replace the cylinder during a planned renovation rather than immediately

## Questions to ask before upgrading

- What is the measured incoming pressure and flow?
- Which existing pipes may need replacement?
- Will fixtures tolerate mains pressure?
- What cylinder capacity is proposed and why?
- How will relief water be drained?
- Is tempering included?
- Who completes electrical work?
- Are leak testing and old-cylinder disposal included?

## What should be included in a conversion quote?

The quote should identify the proposed cylinder, capacity, pressure rating and valve set. It should state whether the old cylinder, roof vent, header tank and redundant pipework will be removed. Electrical alterations, safe-tray drainage, seismic restraint, tempering, pipe insulation and surface reinstatement should be listed separately.

Check whether the quote includes upgrades to old flexible hoses, mixers and concealed pipe sections. No contractor can guarantee every hidden joint, but known incompatible or visibly deteriorated components should not be ignored.

## Testing after conversion

Commissioning should verify:

- Static and operating pressure
- Hot and cold flow
- Relief-valve operation
- Delivery temperature
- Leaks at altered and accessible existing joints
- Performance with several fixtures operating
- Cylinder heating and electrical control

Run showers and mixers cautiously. A sudden pressure increase can expose loose tapware or weak hoses. Report water hammer, temperature fluctuation and leakage immediately.

## Water use after a pressure upgrade

Stronger showers often consume more water per minute. If occupants keep the same shower duration, water and energy use may rise. Flow-efficient shower heads can preserve comfort while limiting consumption, but must remain compatible with the system.

## When to keep the existing system

Retaining low pressure is rational when performance is adequate, the cylinder remains sound and conversion would require extensive pipe replacement. Repairing a control valve or installing compatible tapware may solve the actual complaint at lower cost.

Upgrade because measured performance fails the household's needs, not because a salesperson labels every low-pressure system obsolete.

Record the original and final pressure readings, new valve model and cylinder details. These records help diagnose future faults and prevent another contractor from treating the system as an unknown installation.

Give occupants clear instructions for isolation, normal relief discharge and the expected change in shower flow. Early understanding prevents harmless operation from being reported as failure and helps genuine leaks get noticed quickly.

## Frequently asked questions

### Decision summary

Keep the low-pressure system when it is sound, economical and adequate. Repair restrictions before considering conversion. Upgrade when measured performance no longer meets realistic demand and the rest of the plumbing can tolerate the change.

Obtain at least one quote that separates essential cylinder work from optional fixture and pipe upgrades. A pressure conversion should deliver a documented system design, not simply a different label on the cylinder.

After any upgrade, monitor water use. Higher available flow can increase consumption and empty stored hot water faster. Match shower heads and mixers to the intended balance of comfort, capacity and efficiency.

### Is low-pressure hot water unsafe?

No. A correctly installed and maintained low-pressure system can be safe and effective. Its main limitation is performance, not inherent safety.

### Can I install a mains-pressure shower on a low-pressure cylinder?

Fixture compatibility must be checked. A mixer designed for balanced mains pressure may perform poorly with unequal supplies.

### Does mains pressure increase the power bill?

The cylinder technology and usage matter. Stronger flow may increase hot-water consumption even when the heater efficiency is unchanged.

### Can a pressure pump convert the whole house?

Not as a universal shortcut. System design, source, pipework and compliance must be assessed by an authorised plumber.

### Is a new cylinder always required?

A low-pressure vessel cannot simply be operated above its rated pressure. Conversion normally requires equipment designed for the intended pressure.

### Who performs the conversion?

Restricted plumbing must be carried out within New Zealand's licensing framework, with electrical work completed by an authorised electrical worker.

title: Electric Hot Water Cylinder vs Heat-Pump Water Heater
excerpt: Compare electric resistance cylinders with heat-pump water heaters for efficiency, cost, noise, space, climate and household demand in New Zealand.
content:
An electric resistance cylinder is cheaper and simpler to install, while a heat-pump water heater normally uses much less electricity to heat the same amount of water. A heat-pump system has higher upfront cost, needs suitable space and produces sound and cool exhaust air. The better choice depends on hot-water usage, climate, installation conditions and ownership period.

## How an electric cylinder works

An electric element converts electricity directly into heat inside the stored-water cylinder. The technology is simple, quiet and widely supported.

Advantages:

- Lower purchase cost
- Straightforward replacement
- Quiet operation
- Compact indoor installation options
- Familiar servicing
- Predictable performance

Disadvantages:

- Higher energy use than heat-pump technology
- Standing heat loss from storage
- Element and thermostat faults
- Savings depend heavily on tariff and control schedule

## How a hot-water heat pump works

A heat pump transfers heat from ambient air into water using a refrigeration cycle. It consumes electricity to move heat rather than creating all heat through resistance.

New Zealand's EECA states that hot-water heat pumps can use around one-third of the energy needed by electric resistance or gas water heating under the assumptions in its guidance. Actual savings depend on climate, usage, tariff, system design and backup heating.

Advantages:

- Lower operating energy
- Reduced exposure to future hot-water electricity use
- Strong fit for high-use households
- Can pair with solar electricity generation

Disadvantages:

- Higher purchase and installation cost
- Fan and compressor noise
- Space and airflow requirements
- Condensate drainage
- Reduced efficiency in colder air
- More complex servicing

## Split vs integrated heat-pump systems

An integrated unit places heat-pump components with the cylinder. A split system separates the outdoor unit and storage vessel.

Integrated systems can simplify layout but may be taller and concentrate sound near the cylinder. Split systems allow more flexible component placement but require additional connections and installation planning.

## Running-cost comparison

Do not rely on a universal annual saving. Calculate using:

- Household hot-water energy use
- Electricity tariff
- Heat-pump seasonal efficiency
- Climate zone
- Backup-element operation
- Cylinder standing loss
- Maintenance cost
- Expected ownership period

High-use households have more opportunity to recover the additional purchase cost. Low-use households may face a long payback.

## Climate performance

Heat pumps work by extracting heat from air. Colder ambient temperatures can reduce capacity and efficiency. The product should be selected for the local climate rather than a national average.

Ask for performance data at relevant outdoor temperatures, not only an ideal test condition.

## Noise and placement

Fans and compressors produce sound. Avoid placing equipment directly outside bedrooms or close to neighbours without checking rated sound levels and operating cycles.

The unit also moves air and produces condensate. Enclosing it in an unventilated cupboard can reduce performance and cause moisture problems unless designed for that location.

## Hot-water capacity and recovery

Both technologies must meet peak demand. Compare usable stored volume and recovery rate.

A heat pump may use an electric backup element during high demand or cold conditions. Frequent backup use reduces expected savings.

## Installation requirements

A [heat-pump water heater installation](/heat-pump-water-heater) assessment should include:

- Cylinder capacity
- Outdoor or plant-space suitability
- Airflow
- Noise
- Condensate route
- Pipe length and insulation
- Electrical circuit
- Seismic restraint
- Safe tray and relief drainage
- Access for future service

## Replacement complexity

A like-for-like electric-cylinder replacement may fit the existing cupboard and connections. A heat-pump conversion can require outdoor equipment, new pipe routes, condensate drainage and electrical changes.

Compare complete installed prices rather than appliance retail prices.

## Reliability and maintenance

Electric cylinders have fewer active components. Heat-pump systems include a fan, compressor, sensors and refrigeration controls.

Maintenance needs vary by model. Ask about:

- Local service support
- Parts availability
- Filter or coil cleaning
- Refrigeration warranty
- Cylinder warranty
- Backup operation during repair

## Solar electricity interaction

A programmable heat pump can heat water when rooftop solar production is available, increasing self-consumption. Controls must still maintain safe storage temperatures and adequate supply.

An electric resistance cylinder can also act as flexible storage with timers or diversion controls. The best integration depends on the solar system and household load profile.

## Temperature and hygiene

Government guidance requires cylinder storage at not less than 60°C to control Legionella. Delivery to personal-hygiene fixtures must be tempered to control scalding.

Confirm that the heat pump and its control strategy can reach and maintain required storage conditions, including any periodic sanitation cycle.

## Environmental comparison

Lower electricity use generally reduces operational emissions, but the full comparison includes refrigerant, product manufacture and equipment life. Correct refrigerant handling is essential during service and disposal.

## When an electric cylinder is the practical choice

- Budget is limited
- Hot-water use is low
- No suitable heat-pump location exists
- Noise constraints are strict
- The property will be held briefly
- Simple, rapid replacement is essential

## When a heat pump is the practical choice

- Hot-water use is consistently high
- A suitable installation location exists
- The household expects long ownership
- Energy cost reduction is a priority
- Solar electricity is available
- Product support is credible locally

## Questions to ask installers

- What annual energy assumptions support the savings estimate?
- What is performance in the local climate?
- How often will backup resistance heating operate?
- Where will condensate discharge?
- What sound level applies?
- Who services refrigeration faults?
- Are plumbing and electrical work included?
- What warranties apply separately to tank and compressor?

## Calculate payback without sales assumptions

Ask for the estimated annual hot-water energy before and after installation. The calculation should state electricity price, number of occupants, daily water use, local climate performance and expected backup-element operation.

Divide the additional installed cost by realistic annual savings to estimate simple payback. This does not include financing, maintenance or future tariff changes, but it exposes unrealistic claims. A household using little hot water will not achieve the same savings as a large family.

## Compare complete installed cost

Include:

- Removal of the old cylinder
- New cylinder and heat-pump unit
- Plumbing and valves
- Electrical circuit and controls
- Condensate drainage
- Outdoor base or brackets
- Pipe insulation
- Wall penetrations
- Commissioning
- Warranty and service access

Retail appliance price is not project cost.

## Backup heating matters

Many systems include an electric element for peak demand, sanitation cycles or cold conditions. Ask when it activates and whether the controller displays its operation. A system relying heavily on backup resistance may deliver lower savings than advertised.

## Replacement and refrigerant handling

At end of life, refrigeration components require competent recovery and disposal. Confirm local service support before selecting an uncommon imported model. A cheap system with no parts or technician network creates a future replacement problem.

Request commissioning data and controller instructions. Occupants need to understand boost modes, holiday settings and error codes without disabling required sanitation or temperature controls.

Monitor the first full month of operation against weather and household use before judging savings.

## Frequently asked questions

### Decision summary

Choose a conventional electric cylinder when simplicity, low capital cost, quiet operation and rapid replacement dominate. Choose a heat-pump system when hot-water use is high, the site has suitable airflow and noise separation, and credible energy savings justify the added cost.

Do not compare product efficiency alone. Compare installed cost, seasonal performance, backup-element use, servicing and ownership period. Request the exact model's climate and sound data.

For either system, capacity, pressure, safe storage temperature, tempered delivery and relief drainage remain necessary. Efficient heating does not compensate for incorrect plumbing design.

### Are hot-water heat pumps cheaper to run?

Usually, because they transfer heat efficiently. Actual savings vary with climate, usage, tariff and backup heating.

### Can a heat-pump water heater go inside?

Only where the product and room provide suitable airflow, volume, drainage and noise control. Follow manufacturer requirements.

### Do they work in cold New Zealand regions?

Suitable models can, but efficiency and recovery change with temperature. Select using local-climate performance data.

### Is an electric cylinder maintenance-free?

No. Valves, elements, thermostats and the tank can fail. It is simply less mechanically complex.

### Does a heat pump provide mains pressure?

Pressure depends on the cylinder and plumbing design, not the heat-pump principle alone.

### Who installs the system?

The project may require an authorised plumber, electrical worker and refrigeration technician, depending on the product and scope.

title: Continuous-Flow Gas Hot Water vs Storage Cylinder
excerpt: Compare continuous-flow gas water heating with storage cylinders for supply, energy, space, gas availability, installation and maintenance.
content:
A continuous-flow gas heater warms water when a tap opens and does not store a large hot-water volume. A storage cylinder heats and holds water for later use. Continuous flow saves indoor cylinder space and can supply long draws, but it requires suitable gas, water flow, flue clearances and appliance capacity. Storage provides a reserve but can run out and loses heat while standing.

## How continuous-flow gas works

The unit senses water flow, ignites a gas burner and transfers heat into the moving water. It stops when demand ends.

Advantages:

- No large stored-water tank
- Long hot-water duration within unit capacity
- Reduced storage heat loss
- Outdoor mounting options
- Temperature controllers on compatible systems

Disadvantages:

- Requires gas supply
- Minimum flow may be needed to activate
- Capacity limits simultaneous outlets
- Gas and flue work is regulated
- LPG bottles require management
- Performance can change with incoming water temperature

## How a storage cylinder works

The cylinder heats a fixed volume using electricity, gas, heat pump, solar or another source. Water remains available until usage exceeds storage and recovery.

Advantages:

- Stored reserve
- Multiple energy-source options
- Can use controlled electricity tariffs
- Familiar operation
- Some systems integrate solar or wetback heat

Disadvantages:

- Requires storage space
- Standing heat loss
- Finite supply
- Tank can leak or corrode
- Relief and temperature controls require maintenance

## Hot-water supply

Continuous flow is often described as endless. That is misleading. Duration may be long, but flow is limited by heater capacity, gas input, incoming temperature and simultaneous demand.

A storage cylinder delivers at its designed flow until usable hot water is depleted. Larger storage or faster recovery supports higher demand but increases cost and space.

## Simultaneous bathrooms

A heater rated for one shower may lose temperature or flow when a second shower and kitchen tap operate. Sizing should use realistic simultaneous demand and winter incoming-water temperature.

A correctly sized mains-pressure cylinder may supply several fixtures, but it can empty during sustained use.

## Natural gas vs LPG

Piped natural gas availability is location-dependent. LPG systems use cylinders that must be refilled or exchanged. Running out of LPG stops hot water, and bottle location must meet safety requirements.

Fuel cost comparisons must use current tariffs and actual efficiency. Do not accept a national blanket statement that gas is always cheaper.

## Installation requirements

[Continuous-flow gas installation](/continuous-flow-gas-hot-water) may involve:

- Gas-pipe sizing
- Appliance location
- Flue and clearance requirements
- Water flow and pressure
- Electrical supply for controls and ignition
- Condensate drainage for applicable models
- Temperature controllers
- Certification

All gasfitting requires appropriate authorisation. WorkSafe states that completed connected gasfitting requires a Gas Safety Certificate, with additional compliance certification for defined work categories.

## Space considerations

Removing an indoor cylinder can free a cupboard. The outdoor gas unit still needs a compliant position away from openings and other restrictions. Long pipe runs can increase the time before hot water reaches a tap.

## Water efficiency

Users may run water while waiting for a distant continuous-flow unit. Good pipe routing, insulation and fixture placement matter.

An “endless” supply can also increase shower duration, offsetting energy savings.

## Power outages

Many modern gas continuous-flow heaters need electricity for ignition, controls and fans. They may not operate during a power outage even when gas remains available.

An electric storage cylinder cannot reheat during an outage but retains already heated water for a period.

## Maintenance and faults

Continuous-flow units can develop ignition, sensor, burner, scale, fan or flow problems. Storage systems can develop element, thermostat, valve and tank faults.

Gas-appliance servicing must preserve combustion and flue safety. Use [gas water heater repair](/gas-water-heater-repair), not a general handyman.

## Temperature control

Continuous-flow units often regulate outlet temperature electronically. Storage cylinders keep water hot enough for hygiene control and use tempering where required to limit delivery temperature.

Both systems must provide safe delivery. User-adjustable controls do not replace correct installation.

## Efficiency comparison

Efficiency depends on appliance design, standing loss, pipe loss, usage pattern and fuel. A modern continuous-flow unit avoids tank standing losses. A heat-pump storage system can still use less purchased energy than gas heating.

Compare whole-life cost:

- Appliance and installation
- Gas connection or LPG bottles
- Energy tariffs
- Maintenance
- Expected life
- Replacement parts
- Household usage

## When continuous-flow gas fits

- Gas is already available
- Indoor cylinder space is valuable
- Long-duration demand is common
- Appliance capacity matches simultaneous flow
- A compliant unit location exists
- The household accepts gas-price exposure

## When storage fits

- No gas supply exists
- Simpler electric replacement is preferred
- Solar or heat-pump integration is planned
- A controlled tariff is available
- Stored reserve is valued
- Existing space and pipework suit a cylinder

## Questions to ask before conversion

- Is the existing gas pipe large enough?
- What flow can the unit provide in winter?
- How many fixtures can operate together?
- Where will the appliance be mounted?
- How long are the hot-water pipe runs?
- Is a Gas Safety Certificate included?
- What happens during power failure?
- What are current fuel and servicing costs?

## Temperature drop and distance to fixtures

The heater may produce water at the correct temperature while users still wait because cooled water remains in long pipes. Moving the heater closer, insulating pipes or redesigning distribution can reduce delay. Oversized pipework can hold more stagnant water and extend wait time.

Recirculation is not an automatic solution. It adds pipework, controls and heat loss and must be designed for the system.

## LPG bottle planning

An LPG installation needs compliant bottle location, regulator capacity, changeover arrangement and safe access for replacement. Household users must monitor supply. A single empty bottle at night is an operational failure even when the heater itself is sound.

Compare delivered LPG cost and bottle rental or service charges. Include other gas appliances when sizing the supply.

## Conversion from an electric cylinder

Removing a cylinder may require gas installation, outdoor mounting, new water pipes, electrical control supply, cupboard alterations and removal of redundant vent or tray components. The freed cupboard should not be finished until disconnected pipes and moisture damage are checked.

## Gas certification and records

WorkSafe requires a Gas Safety Certificate for completed connected gasfitting, with Certificates of Compliance applying to general and high-risk work. Keep certificates, appliance model information and commissioning results. A receipt saying “gas heater installed” is not a substitute for required certification.

## Future fuel choice

Consider whether the property will retain gas for cooking or space heating. Installing gas solely for water heating creates connection and maintenance costs that must be compared with heat-pump and electric options.

After installation, test fixtures together, confirm controller temperatures and retain gas certification. Report ignition errors, soot, unusual smell or unstable temperature rather than repeatedly cycling the appliance.

Show every occupant the safe controller settings and gas-isolation procedure. Keep appliance clearances unobstructed and never enclose an outdoor unit later without checking the installation requirements and gasfitter advice.

Schedule servicing according to manufacturer instructions.

## Frequently asked questions

### Decision summary

Continuous-flow gas suits properties with a compliant gas supply, suitable appliance location and demand that fits the unit's winter capacity. Storage suits properties that value a reserve, use electricity or heat-pump technology and have space for a cylinder.

Calculate total installed and operating cost. Include gas connection or LPG service, certificates, maintenance, electrical controls and changes to water pipework. Compare this with an efficient storage alternative.

The correct choice must deliver acceptable flow at the farthest and most demanding fixtures. Ask the installer to state simultaneous-flow assumptions instead of using the vague promise of endless hot water.

### Is continuous-flow hot water unlimited?

Duration can be long, but flow and temperature are limited by appliance capacity and supply conditions.

### Is gas cheaper than an electric cylinder?

Not always. Compare current tariffs, appliance efficiency, standing losses, LPG charges and usage.

### Can one gas unit run two showers?

Only if its rated capacity and site conditions support the combined demand.

### Does a gas heater work during a power cut?

Many modern units require electricity. Check the specific model.

### Can a plumber install the gas heater?

Gasfitting requires the relevant gasfitter authorisation. A person licensed only for plumbing is not automatically licensed for gas work.

### Will removing the cylinder improve pressure?

Not automatically. Supply pressure, pipe size and appliance flow limits determine performance.

title: Common Hot Water Cylinder Problems and Warning Signs
excerpt: Identify common hot water cylinder problems, including leaks, no hot water, low pressure, noise, discolouration and relief-valve discharge.
content:
Common hot water cylinder problems include no heating, insufficient hot water, leaks, low flow, excessive temperature, unusual noise, discoloured water and continuous relief-valve discharge. Some faults involve replaceable components. Leakage from the storage vessel, severe corrosion and recurring failures often justify replacement.

## 1. No hot water

An electric cylinder may have lost power or developed an element, thermostat or wiring fault. A gas storage system may have supply, ignition or burner problems. A heat-pump system can develop control, compressor or backup-element faults.

Check whether every outlet is affected and whether a known outage exists. Do not remove covers or repeatedly reset protection. Use the appropriate plumber, electrician or gasfitter.

## 2. Hot water runs out quickly

Possible causes include:

- Unusually high household demand
- Cylinder capacity too small
- One failed element
- Slow recovery
- Thermostat or control fault
- Cold-water mixing problem
- Sediment or internal deterioration
- A hidden hot-water leak

Compare current performance with previous normal behaviour. A system that always had limited capacity may need resizing; a sudden change indicates a fault.

## 3. Water is only lukewarm

Warm but not hot water can result from a thermostat, element, tempering valve, mixer crossover or heat-source problem.

Do not simply raise the thermostat. New Zealand guidance requires safe storage temperature for Legionella control and controlled delivery temperature to prevent scalding. The storage and mixing functions need testing.

## 4. Water is excessively hot

Excessive outlet temperature creates a scald risk. Possible causes include thermostat failure, incorrect setting, tempering-valve failure or control problems.

Stop vulnerable users from accessing the affected fixtures and arrange urgent assessment. Do not attempt to compensate by changing unrelated valves.

## 5. Cylinder or pipework leaks

Leaks can develop at:

- Inlet or outlet connections
- Valve unions
- Relief pipes
- Element openings
- Tempering valves
- The tank body or seams

External components may be repairable. Tank leakage usually means [hot water cylinder replacement](/hot-water-cylinder-installation).

## 6. Relief pipe discharges continuously

Relief devices protect the cylinder against unsafe pressure or temperature. Continuous discharge may involve a worn valve, debris, pressure-control fault, thermal expansion or overheating.

Never cap or plug a relief pipe. Arrange [hot water cylinder repair](/hot-water-cylinder-repair).

## 7. Low hot-water pressure

Low-pressure cylinders naturally deliver less pressure than mains-pressure systems. A new reduction can indicate a blocked strainer, failing control valve, corroded pipe, partially closed valve or mixer restriction.

Measure pressure and flow before planning an upgrade. See [water-pressure troubleshooting](/water-pressure-upgrades).

## 8. Banging, popping or rumbling

Noise can result from thermal expansion, pipe movement, valve operation, sediment or boiling caused by a control fault. The sound's timing and location matter.

Loud boiling-like noise, overheating or violent pipe movement requires prompt isolation by a professional. Do not assume every sound is harmless “water hammer.”

## 9. Brown or rusty hot water

If only hot water is discoloured, the cylinder or hot-water pipework may be involved. If hot and cold are both affected, the incoming supply or local network is more likely.

Run only enough water to compare safely. Persistent discolouration needs investigation. Tank corrosion combined with leakage supports replacement.

## 10. Bad smell from hot water

Odour can involve water source, stagnation, bacterial activity, anode interaction or pipework. The cause varies by system and water chemistry.

Do not lower storage temperature. A plumber should assess the heater, temperature control and water supply.

## 11. Rising energy bills

Hot-water energy can increase because of:

- A leaking hot tap or pipe
- Continuous relief discharge
- Failed insulation
- Control fault
- Higher usage
- Backup element operating excessively
- A changed tariff

Compare consumption with household activity before blaming the cylinder.

## 12. Water around the cylinder base

Possible sources include tank leakage, a valve above, tray overflow or nearby pipework. Water travels, so trace only visible accessible surfaces without opening electrical covers.

Keep clear of wet electrical components and arrange prompt diagnosis.

## 13. Inconsistent shower temperature

A fluctuating shower may involve the mixer, tempering valve, pressure imbalance, heater recovery or another fixture creating a crossover.

Check whether the problem affects all hot taps. One shower points to a local fixture; the whole house points to a central control or supply issue.

## 14. Slow reheating
