## Heritage and character finishes

Access through original flooring, tiles and joinery can be costly. Compare targeted detection, relining and alternative pipe routes, but do not preserve finishes by leaving unsafe or failed plumbing in place.

## Insurance and records

Insurers may ask about gradual damage and maintenance. Keep inspection reports and evidence of prompt repairs. Accurate records also help future buyers understand which sections were renewed.

## Final ownership strategy

Create a five-year plumbing plan using inspection evidence. Record pipe materials, cylinder age, drain condition and known isolation points. Budget predictable renewal separately from emergencies.

Review the plan after every leak or renovation. If failures begin appearing across several branches, bring forward broader replacement. If the system remains stable, avoid unnecessary demolition driven only by age.

Update plans and photographs whenever concealed pipework is exposed, altered or replaced.

Future repairs become faster, less destructive and easier to quote when routes, materials and isolation points are documented accurately.

## Frequently asked questions

### A staged upgrade plan

Stage one controls active leaks, unsafe gas or electrical interfaces and sewage faults. Stage two replaces high-risk pipes and failed valves while access is available. Stage three improves pressure, capacity and efficiency. Stage four updates fixtures and finishes.

Staging avoids destroying a new bathroom later to replace pipes already known to be deteriorated.

### When repiping may be efficient

Broader replacement becomes rational when leaks occur across several branches, internal corrosion severely restricts flow, most walls are already open or a mains-pressure conversion exposes widespread incompatibility.

Ask for a pipe map, material specification, isolation layout, testing and reinstatement scope. “Repipe house” without boundaries is not a comparable quote.

### When local repair remains sensible

An isolated accessible defect in otherwise sound pipework does not justify full replacement. Keep evidence of surrounding condition and monitor rather than letting fear drive scope.

### Health and safety before cosmetic work

Old properties may contain asbestos or lead-based finishes around access areas. Plumbing contractors should not disturb suspected materials without an appropriate plan. Include hazardous-material assessment in renovation preparation.

### Are old pipes automatically unsafe?

No. Some remain functional. Material, condition, water quality, joints and installation determine risk.

### Should galvanised pipes be replaced?

Replacement is justified when internal corrosion restricts flow, leakage occurs or renovation provides efficient access.

### Can old drains be relined?

Some can when they retain suitable shape and gradient. Collapse and major displacement require excavation.

### Is low-pressure hot water defective?

No. It is a system type. Upgrade when performance no longer meets needs and pipework can support conversion.

### How can illegal alterations be identified?

Check records and have the work assessed by the relevant licensed practitioner. Appearance alone cannot confirm legality.

### What should be inspected before buying an old home?

Water supply, hot water, visible pipes, fixtures, moisture and drainage history. Add CCTV and gas assessment where relevant.

title: Bathroom Renovation Plumbing: What Homeowners Should Plan
excerpt: Plan bathroom plumbing before renovation, including layout, water pressure, drainage, hot-water capacity, waterproofing and licensed trades.
content:
Bathroom plumbing should be planned before demolition and product ordering. Confirm fixture positions, water pressure, hot-water capacity, drain routes, floor levels, ventilation, waterproofing and consent requirements. Moving a toilet or shower can change drainage, structure and cost far more than replacing fixtures in existing positions.

## 1. Define the layout

Mark positions for:

- Toilet
- Shower
- Bath
- Basin and vanity
- Floor waste
- Heated towel rail
- Washing machine where applicable
- Storage and access panels

Existing drain and water routes strongly influence cost. A layout drawing should show dimensions and door clearances, not just inspiration images.

## 2. Decide which fixtures will move

Keeping fixtures in similar positions can reduce pipe alterations. Moving a toilet usually has the greatest drainage impact because of pipe size, gradient and venting.

An authorised plumber and drainlayer should assess feasibility before the design is fixed.

## 3. Check water pressure and flow

Modern mixers have specified pressure ranges. A product designed for balanced mains pressure may perform poorly in a low-pressure home.

Test:

- Hot and cold pressure
- Flow with simultaneous fixtures
- Existing cylinder type
- Pipe size
- Control valves

Plan [water-pressure upgrades](/water-pressure-upgrades) before buying tapware.

## 4. Confirm hot-water capacity

A larger shower head, second bathroom or deep bath can increase demand. Check cylinder storage and recovery.

Options may include:

- Retaining the current cylinder
- Increasing storage capacity
- Converting to mains pressure
- Heat-pump water heating
- Continuous-flow gas where suitable

Do not select a system from fixture count alone; usage and simultaneous demand matter.

## 5. Select compatible fixtures

Check:

- Pressure rating
- Flow requirements
- Connection sizes
- Rough-in dimensions
- Waste position
- Spare-part support
- Lead-free product requirements
- Warranty conditions

Cheap imported tapware without suitable documentation can create compliance and replacement problems.

## 6. Plan toilet drainage

Toilet pans have specific outlet positions and connector requirements. Moving the toilet may require floor cutting, drain alteration and venting changes.

[Toilet installation](/toilet-installation-repair) must leave the pan stable, sealed and accessible for maintenance.

## 7. Plan shower drainage

The shower needs appropriate waste capacity, fall and waterproofing integration. A tiled shower adds membrane, substrate and set-down requirements beyond plumbing alone.

Linear drains do not eliminate the need for correct floor falls and accessible cleaning.

## 8. Coordinate waterproofing

Plumbing penetrations must integrate with the wet-area membrane. Late pipe changes can damage completed waterproofing.

Use documented systems and coordinate plumber, builder, waterproofer and tiler. A leaking shower may be a membrane problem even when plumbing connections are sound.

## 9. Ventilation and condensation

Bathrooms need effective moisture removal. Poor ventilation produces mould and surface damage that may be mistaken for plumbing leakage.

Plan fan capacity, duct route, exterior discharge and make-up air before the ceiling closes.

## 10. Access for maintenance

Concealed cisterns, spa baths and built-in mixers require service access. Do not tile over components that manufacturers require to remain accessible.

Record photos of pipe routes before linings are installed.

## 11. Isolation valves

Accessible fixture or branch isolation reduces disruption during future repairs. Valve type and location should suit the system and remain reachable after cabinetry installation.

## 12. Floor and structural constraints

Drilling or notching joists for wastes can weaken structure. Concrete floors may contain reinforcing, heating or services.

Coordinate pipe routes with structural requirements rather than cutting first and asking later.

## 13. Consent and restricted work

Consent needs depend on project scope. Cosmetic replacement differs from altered sanitary plumbing, drainage, structure or waterproofing.

Almost all sanitary plumbing is restricted in New Zealand. Use a currently licensed practitioner and confirm who handles consent, inspection and producer documentation.

## 14. Electrical coordination

Heated towel rails, lighting, underfloor heating, fans and outlets have wet-area electrical requirements. Use an authorised electrical worker.

Do not position plumbing and electrical components independently.

## 15. Budget structure

Separate allowances for:

- Demolition
- Plumbing and drainage
- Fixtures
- Building work
- Waterproofing
- Electrical
- Ventilation
- Tiling
- Painting
- Consent and inspection
- Waste removal
- Contingency

A low fixture allowance can make a quote look cheaper without reducing final cost.

## Common planning mistakes

- Buying incompatible tapware
- Moving the toilet after work begins
- Ignoring hot-water capacity
- No service access
- Underestimating wall repair
- Assuming every pipe can stay
- Installing oversized shower outlets on weak supply
- Failing to coordinate membrane penetrations
- Using unlicensed labour
- No contingency for concealed damage

## Questions for the plumber

- Can existing pipes support the layout?
- Will drainage gradient work?
- Is hot-water capacity adequate?
- Are selected fixtures pressure-compatible?
- Which walls and floors must open?
- Is consent required?
- Who coordinates drainlaying and electrical work?
- What testing occurs before linings close?
- What documentation will be supplied?

## Pre-lining inspection

Before walls and floors close:

- Check pipe support
- Confirm fixture positions
- Test water connections
- Inspect waste gradients
- Photograph routes
- Confirm penetrations
- Verify access panels
- Complete required inspections

Fixing errors is cheaper before waterproofing and tiles.

## Construction sequence

A typical coordinated sequence is:

1. Finalise design and fixture specifications.
2. Confirm consent and trade responsibilities.
3. Isolate and demolish safely.
4. Complete framing and structural changes.
5. Rough in water, wastes and electrical services.
6. Inspect and test concealed work.
7. Install substrate and waterproofing.
8. Tile and finish surfaces.
9. Fit and connect fixtures.
10. Test, commission and document.

Changing fixture models after rough-in can shift waste and mixer positions and damage completed work.

## Quote comparison checklist

Ensure each quote states:

- Exact fixtures or allowances
- Plumbing rough-in and fit-off
- Drain alterations
- Builder's work
- Waterproofing system
- Electrical work
- Ventilation
- Consent and inspections
- Demolition and disposal
- Surface repair
- Exclusions and variation rates

A quote missing waterproofing, electrical or reinstatement is not cheaper; it is incomplete.

## Practical design choices

Wall-mounted tapware looks clean but creates concealed components that require precise framing and future access. Concealed cisterns save space but need service panels. Freestanding baths can complicate cleaning and waste access. Large showers consume more hot water and require correct drainage.

Choose products based on serviceability as well as appearance.

## Final handover checks

- Run every tap and shower
- Flush the toilet repeatedly
- Fill and drain the basin and bath
- Check cabinetry for leaks
- Confirm shower falls and drainage
- Verify stable hot-water temperature
- Test fans and heated equipment
- Receive warranties and approvals
- Photograph completed service access

Do not make final payment while active leaks or incomplete documentation remain unresolved.

Inspect the bathroom again after several weeks of normal use. Check cabinetry, seals, drainage and ventilation before minor defects create hidden damage.

Record any defect immediately.

## Frequently asked questions

### Product-order schedule

Order concealed components, wastes, mixers and specialty fixtures early enough for rough-in. Confirm actual model numbers before pipes are positioned. A catalogue drawing can change between variants.

Inspect deliveries for damage and missing parts. Do not install a cracked pan, warped bath or incomplete concealed mixer body merely to maintain schedule.

### Variation control

Require written price and time impact before changing layout, fixtures or finishes. Moving one waste after waterproofing can trigger demolition, new membrane work and inspection.

Keep a decision log with drawings and product links. Verbal site changes create disputes.

### Accessibility and future occupants

Consider step-free shower entry, grab-bar backing, toilet clearances, lever controls and anti-scald protection even when immediate occupants do not need them. Structural backing is cheap before walls close and expensive later.

### Maintenance handover

Learn how to isolate fixtures, clean wastes and access concealed cisterns. Keep spare tile information, grout and sealant specifications, waterproofing warranty and plumber details. Schedule seal and ventilation checks before visible water damage develops.

### Is moving a toilet expensive?

It can be because the larger drain needs correct fall, venting and structural coordination. Site access determines the cost.

### Can any mixer work with low pressure?

No. Check the manufacturer's minimum and balanced-pressure requirements.

### Does replacing a shower require consent?

It depends on whether plumbing, drainage, waterproofing or structure changes. Confirm with the council and licensed tradesperson.

### Should the cylinder be upgraded during renovation?

Only if pressure, capacity, condition or efficiency no longer meets the planned bathroom demand.

### Who waterproofs around plumbing penetrations?

Responsibility must be coordinated between plumber, builder and membrane installer using the selected system details.

### What records should be kept?

Plans, photos, licences, approvals, product documents, inspection records, warranties and invoices.
